title: Migrate .golangci.yml to v2 format and update CI workflow
description:
## Description
Migrate the golangci-lint configuration from v1 to v2 schema format and update the
CI workflow to use a golangci-lint version compatible with Go 1.25.6. This is the
foundational task that fixes the CI bug (ai-config-manager-k6o8).

## Context
- Current config uses v1 format (`linters.enable`, `linters-settings`)
- v2 format uses `version: "2"`, `linters.default`, `linters.settings` (no hyphen)
- v2 format uses `linters.exclusions.rules` instead of `issues.exclude-rules`
- CI currently uses `golangci/golangci-lint-action@v3` with `version: latest`
- The `latest` version is built with Go 1.24, incompatible with our Go 1.25.6

## Instructions

### Step 1: Rewrite .golangci.yml to v2 format

The v2 format has these structural changes from v1:
- Add `version: "2"` at the top
- `linters-settings:` → `linters.settings:` (nested under `linters:`)
- `issues:` → `linters.exclusions:` 
- `exclude-rules:` → `linters.exclusions.rules:`
- `exclude-use-default: false` → `linters.exclusions.preset: none` (or remove)
- `max-issues-per-linter: 0` / `max-same-issues: 0` → `output.uniq-by-line: true`
- `linters.enable:` stays but add `default: none` to prevent defaults

Rewrite the current config preserving all EXISTING linter rules (don't add/remove
linters yet — that's the next task). The goal is a format-only migration.

Reference the beads project config structure:
```yaml
version: "2"

run:
  timeout: 5m

linters:
  default: 'none'
  enable:
    - errcheck
    - gosec
    # ... etc

  settings:
    errcheck:
      exclude-functions:
        # ...

  exclusions:
    rules:
      - path: '_test\.go'
        linters:
          - dupl
      # ...
```

Keep all 10 current linters: govet, staticcheck, errcheck, gosec, gocritic,
dupl, gocyclo, goconst, misspell, unconvert.

Keep all current settings (dupl threshold 100, gocyclo min 15, goconst min-len 3
min-occurrences 3, gosec excludes G304/G301/G306, errcheck excludes Close).

Keep all current exclusion rules (dupl in tests, gosec G204 in tests).

Add `run.timeout: 5m` (v2 best practice, prevents CI timeouts).

### Step 2: Update CI workflow (.github/workflows/build.yml)

Change the golangci-lint step:
```yaml
- name: golangci-lint
  uses: golangci/golangci-lint-action@v7
  with:
    version: latest
    args: --timeout=5m
```

Key change: `@v3` → `@v7` (v7 supports golangci-lint v2 and Go 1.25+).

### Step 3: Validate locally

Run `golangci-lint run --timeout=5m` locally to verify the v2 config parses
correctly and produces the same results as the v1 config.

## Files to Modify
- .golangci.yml (rewrite to v2 format)
- .github/workflows/build.yml (update action version)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] .golangci.yml uses `version: "2"` at the top
- [ ] Config uses v2 key names (settings, exclusions, etc.)
- [ ] All 10 existing linters still enabled
- [ ] All existing settings preserved (thresholds, excludes)
- [ ] All existing exclusion rules preserved
- [ ] CI workflow uses golangci-lint-action@v7
- [ ] `golangci-lint run` passes locally with v2 config
- [ ] No new lint violations introduced

## Notes
- The v2 migration guide: https://golangci-lint.run/product/migration-guide/
- This task deliberately does NOT change which linters are enabled or their settings
- Linter rule changes happen in the next task to keep changes reviewable
close_reason: v2 migration complete: .golangci.yml rewritten, CI action updated to v7

title: Fix: Make config optional with sensible defaults
description:
## Description
13 integration tests are failing because `aimgr install` requires a config file to exist at `~/.config/aimgr/aimgr.yaml`. When no config exists, `LoadGlobal()` returns an error instead of providing sensible defaults.

## Failing Tests
- TestZeroArgInstall
- TestSaveOnInstall
- TestNoSaveFlag
- TestNoSaveFlagWithExistingManifest
- TestMissingResources
- TestBackwardCompatibility
- TestInstallFromManifestVsArgs
- TestManifestPersistence
- TestManifestErrorRecovery
- TestCLIInstall
- TestCLIInstallMultiple
- TestCLIUninstall

## Root Cause
In `pkg/config/config.go`, `LoadGlobal()` function returns an error when no config file exists (lines 194-201).

This is called in `cmd/install.go` at multiple locations when no `--target` flag is provided.

## Expected Behavior
When no config file exists:
1. Tool should work out-of-the-box without requiring configuration
2. Should use sensible defaults (e.g., claude as default target)
3. Only require config for advanced usage

## Proposed Solution
Modify `LoadGlobal()` to return a default config when no file exists:

```go
// Default config when none exists
defaultConfig := &Config{
    Install: InstallConfig{
        Targets: []string{"claude"},  // Sensible default
    },
}
return defaultConfig, nil
```

## Files to Fix
- `pkg/config/config.go` - Modify `LoadGlobal()` to return defaults

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `LoadGlobal()` returns default config when no file exists
- [ ] All 13 failing integration tests pass
- [ ] CI is green
- [ ] Tool works without requiring config file creation
- [ ] Default target is "claude" when no config exists
close_reason: Fixed: LoadGlobal() now returns default config with claude as default target. All 13 integration tests passing.

title: Fix TestDiscoverSkills_EmptyRepo test failure
description:
## Description

Unit test `TestDiscoverSkills_EmptyRepo` is failing:

```
skills_test.go:144: expected 0 skills in empty repo, got 8
```

## Root Cause

The test expects an empty repository to have 0 skills, but it's finding 8 skills. This suggests:

1. Test is not properly creating an empty repo (test isolation issue)
2. Test is picking up skills from the project directory itself
3. Discovery logic is finding skills where it shouldn't

## Files to Check

- `pkg/discovery/skills_test.go` - Line 144
- Discovery logic that might be recursing too far

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Test passes: `go test -v ./pkg/discovery -run TestDiscoverSkills_EmptyRepo`
- [ ] Test properly isolates and creates empty temp directory
- [ ] Test doesn't leak into project directory
close_reason: Fixed by using t.TempDir() for test isolation

title: Fix golangci-lint Go version incompatibility
description:
## Description

CI linting fails with Go version mismatch error:

```
Error: can't load config: the Go language version (go1.24) used to build golangci-lint 
is lower than the targeted Go version (1.25.6)
```

## Root Cause

The project uses Go 1.25.6, but golangci-lint v1.64.8 is built with Go 1.24.

## Solution Options

1. **Downgrade Go version** in `go.mod` to 1.24 (not recommended - loses features)
2. **Pin golangci-lint version** that supports Go 1.25.6
3. **Wait for golangci-lint update** that supports Go 1.25.6
4. **Use different linter** or disable version check temporarily

## Files to Check

- `go.mod` - current Go version
- `.github/workflows/lint.yml` - golangci-lint configuration
- `.golangci.yml` - linter config

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] CI lint workflow passes
- [ ] No Go version mismatch errors
- [ ] Linting still works correctly
close_reason: Fixed by updating CI to use Go 1.25.6 and golangci-lint v2.9.0

title: BUG: Compile error in cmd/repo_import.go - absPath undefined
description:
## Description
Commit 067cde9 introduced a compile error in cmd/repo_import.go. The `absPath` variable was moved inside an `if` block (line 598) but is still referenced outside the block (line 615).

## Root Cause
The commit attempted to add format guards to suppress header output for JSON/YAML formats:
```go
// Line 597: Guard was added here
if importFormatFlag == "" || importFormatFlag == "table" {
    absPath, _ := filepath.Abs(localPath)  // line 598 - absPath scoped to this block
    fmt.Printf("Importing from: %s\n", absPath)
    ...
}

// Line 615: Still references absPath from OUTSIDE the block
sourceURL := "file://" + absPath  // COMPILE ERROR: undefined absPath
```

## Impact
- **Binary cannot be rebuilt** from current source
- Tests run against stale binary from before the broken commit
- All test failures are based on stale binary behavior, not current code
- The format guards added in commit 067cde9 have NEVER been tested

## To Reproduce
```bash
make build
# Output: cmd/repo_import.go:615:27: undefined: absPath
```

## Fix Required
1. Move `absPath` declaration BEFORE the `if` block so it's available at line 615
2. Keep the format guard for headers (the intent of the original change was correct)

```go
// Correct fix:
absPath, _ := filepath.Abs(localPath)

// Print header (LOCAL-SPECIFIC) - only for table format
if importFormatFlag == "" || importFormatFlag == "table" {
    fmt.Printf("Importing from: %s\n", absPath)
    ...
}

// Now absPath is in scope
sourceURL := "file://" + absPath
```

## Files to Modify
- cmd/repo_import.go (line 598: move absPath declaration before if block)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `make build` succeeds without errors
- [ ] `make vet` passes
- [ ] Format guards work correctly (no headers in yaml/json output)
close_reason: Moved absPath declaration before format guard if-block to fix scoping issue. make build succeeds. The vet failure in list_test.go is a pre-existing issue in a different file.

title: BUG: TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageWithMissingRefs fails due to responsive table output
description:
## Description
Test `TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageWithMissingRefs` in `test/repo_verify_test.go` is failing due to output format changes from responsive table rendering.

## Error
```
Expected '3 missing' count in output, got: Repository Verification
...
│ test-package │ 3             │ command/missing-cmd, skill/missing-skill, agent/missing-agent │
```

The test expects the string "3 missing" but the new table format shows "3" in the MISSING COUNT column.

## Root Cause
The responsive table implementation (v1.22.0) changed the table output format. Test assertions were not updated to match the new format.

## To Reproduce
```bash
go test -v ./test -run TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageWithMissingRefs
```

## Expected Behavior
Test should pass with updated assertion to match new table format.

## Files Affected
- test/repo_verify_test.go (around line 96)

## Fix Required
1. Change assertion from checking for "3 missing" to just checking that the package appears in output
2. Or check for "3" in the MISSING COUNT column
3. Verify test passes

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Test passes: `go test -v ./test -run TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageWithMissingRefs`
- [ ] No changes to production code (test-only fix)
- [ ] Assertion checks for correct data in new table format

## Related
Part of responsive table implementation epic (ai-config-manager-ea7).
Test assertions need updating to match new output format.
close_reason: Tests are now PASSING - fixes were already applied

This is NOT related to responsive tables. FIX: Change test from runAimgr(t, 'repo', 'list', '--type=package') to runAimgr(t, 'repo', 'list', 'package/*').

title: BUG: TestOutputFormatYAML fails due to responsive table output format
description:
## Description
Test TestOutputFormatYAML in test/output_format_test.go fails because the aimgr binary outputs non-YAML header lines to stdout when --format=yaml is used.

## Actual Root Cause (Corrected)
This is NOT a responsive table issue. Three compounding problems:

1. **Compile error (ai-config-manager-4wl)**: Commit 067cde9 introduced a compile error preventing binary rebuild. The absPath variable was moved inside an if block but is still referenced outside.
2. **Stale binary**: Tests run against the old binary (built 12:18, before the broken commit at 12:36), which has NO format guards at all.
3. **Incomplete guards**: Even when the compile error is fixed, the importFromLocalPathWithMode function prints 'Found: X commands...' to stdout regardless of format.

## Error Output
```
yaml: line 2: mapping values are not allowed in this context
Output: Importing from: /tmp/...
  Import Mode: SYMLINK (local paths linked, not copied)

Found: 0 commands, 1 skills, 0 agents, 0 packages

added:
  - name: yaml-test-skill
```
The test tries to yaml.Unmarshal the entire output. The leading non-YAML lines cause parse failure.

## Fix Required
1. Fix compile error (ai-config-manager-4wl)
2. Guard ALL informational output for non-table formats (ai-config-manager-2cx)
3. Rebuild binary
4. Test should then pass without test code changes

## Dependencies
- Depends on ai-config-manager-4wl (compile error fix)
- Depends on ai-config-manager-2cx (output guard fix)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Test passes: go test -v ./test -run TestOutputFormatYAML
- [ ] YAML output is parseable without stripping headers
- [ ] JSON test (TestOutputFormatJSON) continues to pass
notes: CORRECTED ROOT CAUSE: This test fails because (1) the binary is stale - commit 067cde9 introduced a compile error preventing rebuild, so tests run against pre-fix binary; (2) the pre-fix binary prints header lines to stdout even in YAML mode; (3) additionally, the 'Found:' informational line in importFromLocalPathWithMode is never guarded by format check. FIX: Fix compile error (ai-config-manager-4wl), guard ALL informational output for non-table formats, rebuild binary.
close_reason: Test passes after fixing compile error (4wl) and adding output format guards (2cx). No test code changes needed. Both TestOutputFormatYAML and TestOutputFormatJSON pass successfully.

title: CRITICAL: Tests polluting real repository - fix test isolation
description:
## Description
CRITICAL BUG: Tests are writing to the real repository at `/home/hans/.local/share/ai-config/repo/` instead of using isolated temporary directories.

## Problem
Tests in `test/bulk_add_cli_test.go` and potentially others are:
1. Not properly isolating the repository path
2. Writing test data to the production repository
3. Polluting the user's real data

## Evidence
Test failure shows:
```
failed to create symlink: symlink /tmp/TestCLIBulkAdd.../resources/commands/bulk-cmd1.md /home/hans/.local/share/ai-config/repo/commands/bulk-cmd1.md: file exists
```

The path `/home/hans/.local/share/ai-config/repo/` is the REAL repository, not a temp directory!

## Root Cause
Tests set `XDG_DATA_HOME` but the code is not respecting it, or there's an environment variable override happening.

## Required Fix
1. Audit ALL test files for repository path usage
2. Ensure tests use `AIMGR_REPO_PATH` environment variable to override repository location
3. Verify ALL tests use `t.TempDir()` for repository isolation
4. Add test helper function to ensure isolation
5. Run full test suite to verify no pollution

## Files to Check
- test/bulk_add_cli_test.go
- test/repo_*.go
- Any test that calls `repo.NewManager()` or similar

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All tests use isolated temporary directories
- [ ] No test writes to `/home/hans/.local/share/ai-config/`
- [ ] Test helper function ensures isolation
- [ ] Full test suite passes without touching real repo
- [ ] Documentation added about test isolation requirements
close_reason: Test fixes completed: isolation fixed, assertions updated

title: Surface override state and verify sync metadata stability
description:
## Description
Make active overrides visible in repo UX and prove that sync, metadata ownership, manifest sharing semantics, and orphan cleanup remain stable across override and restore flows.

## Instructions
1. Update a read-only repo surface to show active overrides clearly, preferably `repo info`, unless another read path is materially better and documented.
2. Decide and document how override state should appear in `repo show-manifest`. If override breadcrumbs are intentionally persisted in `ai.repo.yaml`, make that user-facing behavior explicit and documented. If they are intentionally hidden from shareable output, ensure that behavior is consistent and testable.
3. Add integration coverage for: remote baseline -> local override -> sync -> local changes reflected; then clear -> sync -> original remote restored.
4. Verify source ownership behavior remains correct for `repo sync`, orphan cleanup, and source metadata matching while source transport changes but source name stays stable.
5. Verify override state does not create surprising behavior for `repo remove`, `repo drop-source`, `repo show-manifest`, `repo apply-manifest`, and `repo info`, and document any intentional constraints.
6. Specifically test and document the chosen behavior for removing an overridden source and for sharing/committing manifests while an override is active, so users are not surprised by local-path leakage or hidden restore state.
7. Update user docs to document the supported dev-testing workflow, constraints, recovery steps, and why overlay manifests are not the supported mechanism.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/repo_info.go` — surface override status/details in human and structured output if adopted there
- `cmd/repo_show_manifest.go` and/or related manifest output tests if show-manifest semantics need explicit handling
- `cmd/repo_sync.go` and related tests — verify removal/metadata behavior across override/clear flows
- `cmd/repo_remove.go` and related tests — verify removing overridden sources behaves as documented
- `docs/user-guide/sources.md`, `docs/user-guide/getting-started.md`, `docs/reference/troubleshooting.md`
- Integration/E2E tests under `test/...`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Users can see when a source is overridden and what remote it will restore to.
- [ ] `repo show-manifest` behavior for overridden sources is explicit, documented, and tested.
- [ ] Sync/removal behavior remains correct across override and restore.
- [ ] Docs describe the supported dev-testing workflow, shareability implications, and recovery steps.
- [ ] Integration/E2E tests pass using temp repos and the local binary.
- [ ] Non-overridden source behavior remains unchanged.
close_reason: Implemented override visibility in repo info, explicit shareable show-manifest projection, overridden-source remove semantics, focused sync/ownership tests, and user docs for override workflow and constraints

title: Add repo override-source command with clear and auto-sync flow
description:
## Description
Add a dedicated CLI command that switches a named remote source to a local checkout for testing and restores the original remote definition on demand, without forcing users through manual drop/add workflows.

## Instructions
1. Add a new subcommand under `aimgr repo`: `override-source <source-name> local:/path` and `override-source <source-name> --clear`.
2. Require lookup by stable manifest source name only. Reject unknown sources, override targets that are not `local:` paths, attempts to override unsupported source shapes, attempts to override already-local sources (unless the final design explicitly supports that), and `--clear` on sources that are not currently overridden.
3. Define and implement explicit argument validation for ambiguous invocations, especially combinations like `override-source <name> --clear local:/path` or missing source names.
4. On override, preserve source name/include filters, capture the original remote definition into the chosen persisted override breadcrumb state, replace the active source with the local path/symlink definition, persist the manifest/state atomically, and run sync automatically.
5. On clear, restore the original remote URL/ref/subpath, remove override breadcrumb fields, persist the manifest/state atomically, and run sync automatically.
6. Handle partial-failure surfaces explicitly. If state is persisted but auto-sync fails, return a clear error that tells the user which source definition is now active, whether restore metadata is still intact, and what command to run next.
7. Make command output and errors explicit enough for humans and automated tests, including which source is active after the operation.
8. Add command-level tests covering success, invalid input, clear/restore, double-override policy, override-of-already-local policy, and sync-failure surfaces.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/repo_override_source.go` (create) — command implementation and CLI UX
- `cmd/repo.go` — register the new command
- `cmd/repo_add.go` and/or shared source parsing helpers — reuse or extract local-source parsing/validation if needed
- `cmd/repo_sync.go` and shared helpers if command-triggered sync needs stable error/reporting behavior
- Command/integration tests under `cmd/...` or `test/...`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Users can override a named remote source with a local checkout in one command.
- [ ] Users can restore the original remote with `--clear`.
- [ ] Source name and include filters survive override and restore.
- [ ] Ambiguous or unsupported invocations fail with explicit guidance.
- [ ] The command holds the repo lock, saves state atomically, and syncs automatically.
- [ ] If auto-sync fails after state change, the error explains the resulting active state and next recovery step.
- [ ] Success and error paths are covered by tests.
close_reason: Implemented repo override-source with clear flow, strict validation, persisted override/restore state, auto-sync integration, sync-failure recovery messaging, and command-level tests

title: Persist override metadata and manifest merge identity for overridden sources
description:
## Description
Extend repository source state so a remote source can be overridden locally without losing the original remote definition, while preserving stable source identity for sync/orphan detection and making manifest merge logic treat the override as representing that original upstream for conflict detection.

## Instructions
1. Make an explicit persistence design decision and implement it consistently: override restore breadcrumbs must survive process restarts and `--clear`, and the task must document whether they live in `ai.repo.yaml`, `.metadata/sources.json`, or both. The chosen design must keep local runtime state vs shareable manifest semantics understandable.
2. Extend the relevant source state model with the persisted fields needed to restore the original remote URL/ref/subpath exactly after local override. Preserve source name and include filters; do not start persisting runtime-only source IDs as shareable manifest requirements.
3. Update validation, cloning, load/save, and any migration helpers so overridden sources round-trip cleanly and invalid combinations fail fast with clear errors. Explicitly cover the current `path` XOR `url` validation rule and allow only the override breadcrumb fields needed for restore.
4. Add helper logic for canonical identity / remote-origin identity so `repo apply-manifest` compares incoming shared-manifest entries against the original remote definition when a local override is active, instead of hashing only the active local path.
5. Preserve stable source identity across override and clear for sync/orphan detection. Audit `GenerateSourceID`, manifest load/migration behavior, sync canonical-source helpers, and `.metadata/sources.json` interactions so switching transport does not make previously synced resources look orphaned.
6. Ensure any clone helpers used by merge/apply logic copy override fields so state is not silently lost during apply-manifest operations.
7. Add focused unit tests for validation, save/load round-trip, clone behavior, canonical identity behavior, stable source identity across override/clear, and `repo apply-manifest` conflict/no-op behavior with overridden sources.

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go` — extend source schema, validation, migrations, and round-trip behavior
- `pkg/repomanifest/apply_merge.go` — compare incoming sources against original remote identity when overridden
- `pkg/repomanifest/sourceid.go` and/or related identity helpers — preserve stable source identity across override/clear if needed
- `pkg/sourcemetadata/metadata.go` and related call sites — keep source metadata aligned with the chosen override-state design
- Relevant tests under `pkg/repomanifest/...`, `pkg/sourcemetadata/...`, `cmd/...`, and `test/...`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Overridden sources persist enough remote info to restore URL/ref/subpath exactly after restart.
- [ ] Invalid override states are rejected by validation with clear errors.
- [ ] `repo apply-manifest` no longer conflicts when the incoming source matches the stored original remote of an overridden local source.
- [ ] Overriding and clearing a source does not cause source-id churn that misclassifies existing resources as orphans.
- [ ] Clone/merge paths preserve override state correctly.
- [ ] Existing non-overridden manifest behavior remains unchanged.
- [ ] Targeted unit tests pass.
close_reason: Implemented override breadcrumb persistence in source metadata, override-aware canonical identity/source ID handling, validation/clone/load-save updates, and focused tests for apply-manifest + sync identity stability

title: Add repo override-source command for temporary local source testing
description:
## Description
Add a first-class `aimgr repo override-source` workflow that lets users temporarily override a named remote source with a local checkout for development/testing, then restore the original remote with a single command.

## Goals
- Replace the current multi-step drop/add/sync workaround with an explicit supported command.
- Keep override state reversible, visible, and compatible with `repo sync` and `repo apply-manifest`.
- Preserve stable source ownership semantics so metadata/orphan cleanup stays correct during override and restore.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr repo override-source <name> local:/path` overrides a remote source with one command.
- [ ] `aimgr repo override-source <name> --clear` restores the original remote definition.
- [ ] Active overrides do not create spurious `repo apply-manifest` conflicts for the same upstream source.
- [ ] Sync/resource metadata/orphan cleanup behave correctly during override and restore.
- [ ] User-facing docs and automated tests cover the supported workflow and edge cases.
close_reason: Acceptance review passed; repo override-source feature implemented and verified

description:

title: make test fails on main due to backward-compatibility install assertion
description:
## Summary
Release-readiness verification for `ai-config-manager-tnsr` found the documented test gate failing on current `main`.

## Reproduction
- `make test`
- `go test ./test -run 'TestBackwardCompatibility/zero-arg_install_without_manifest_shows_helpful_error' -v`
- `CGO_ENABLED=1 go test -race ./...`

## Observed
`make test` failed in `integration-test` with:
- `--- FAIL: TestBackwardCompatibility/zero-arg_install_without_manifest_shows_helpful_error`
- `FAIL github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3/test`

Targeted reproduction showed the assertion expects both `not found` and `no resources`, but the actual CLI output is:
`Error: no resources specified and neither ai.package.yaml nor ai.package.local.yaml found`

The race-enabled suite also failed because of the same test failure.

## Impact
`docs/RELEASING.md` and the contributor release checklist require `make test` to pass before release. Current `main` does not meet that requirement.
close_reason: Fixed failing backward-compatibility assertion to match current install error message; verified targeted test and make test pass

title: Shared manifest re-apply ignores updated source ref/tag
description:
## Summary
Re-applying a shared ai.repo.yaml with the same source name and URL but a different ref/tag leaves the existing ref unchanged instead of updating it or surfacing a conflict.

## Why this matters
Teams using pinned or tagged shared manifests need routine ref updates to propagate when they publish a new baseline. Today, users remain pinned to the old ref after re-applying the updated manifest, which breaks the shared-manifest update workflow.

## Reproduction
1. Create manifest v1 with source name team-tools, URL https://github.com/example/tools, ref v1.2.0.
2. Apply it with ./aimgr repo apply-manifest <v1>.
3. Create manifest v2 with the same source name and URL but ref v1.3.0.
4. Apply v2 with ./aimgr repo apply-manifest <v2>.
5. Inspect ./aimgr repo show-manifest.

## Expected
The local manifest should reflect the updated ref, or apply-manifest should fail with explicit guidance.

## Actual
repo apply-manifest reports a noop (identical source already configured) and keeps ref v1.2.0.

## Notes
pkg/repomanifest.MergeForApply currently compares canonical source identity and include filters but does not account for ref/tag changes for same-name sources.
close_reason: Fixed apply-manifest merge logic to treat same-name canonical sources with changed ref as updates and added regression tests for merge and command paths.

title: Harden shared-manifest collision handling to keep repos valid
description:
## Description
Harden collision handling around centrally shared manifests so `aimgr repo apply-manifest` and related repo workflows do not leave users with an invalid or confusing local repository.

This is the main **repo-safety / hardening** task for the epic.

## Instructions
1. Fix duplicate canonical source handling during manifest apply using canonical source identity, not only source name.
2. Also handle the case where different sources expose the same canonical resource name (for example `skill/test-skill` from two different repos) in the relevant repo workflow with an explicit, actionable error.
3. Ensure failures keep the local repo valid; a rejected apply/sync must not persist a broken or ambiguous state.
4. Improve diagnostics so errors clearly identify the conflicting sources/resources and explain what the user should change.
5. Add regression coverage for duplicate-canonical-source cases and conflicting-resource-name-across-sources cases.

## Likely Files / Areas
- `pkg/repomanifest/apply_merge.go`
- `pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go`
- `cmd/repo_apply.go`
- `cmd/repo_sync.go`
- `cmd/repo_apply_test.go`
- `cmd/repo_sync_test.go`
- `pkg/repomanifest/apply_merge_test.go`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Applying a manifest cannot create duplicate canonical sources under different names
- [ ] Conflicting resource names across different sources are detected and surfaced clearly
- [ ] Collision errors name the involved source(s) and resource(s) and give actionable guidance
- [ ] Failed collision scenarios do not leave the local repo in an invalid state
- [ ] Regression tests cover same-URL/different-name, same-path/different-name, and different-source/same-resource-name cases
close_reason: Implemented canonical source collision checks in apply, added sync precheck for cross-source resource-name conflicts, and added regression tests to ensure failed apply/sync leave repo state valid.

title: Decide onboarding workflow for central shared-manifest bootstrap
description:
## Description
Decide the onboarding workflow once teams use centrally published shared setup manifests.

## Decision
The reliable first workflow is explicit bootstrap:

```bash
aimgr repo apply-manifest <central-manifest-url>
aimgr repo sync
aimgr install
```

This explicit flow should be the recommended path in project README files and CI.

## Implications
- a dedicated interactive bootstrap command is **not required** for the first reliable team solution
- install-time prompting/guidance is **optional follow-up work**, not part of the initial required workflow
- CI can keep using explicit commands and does not depend on any interactive UX

## In Scope
- decide the default user-facing onboarding flow
- clarify local developer vs CI behavior

## Explicitly Out of Scope
- advanced install-time prompting or automatic repo mutation UX in the first milestone

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Explicit bootstrap is the default onboarding and CI model
- [x] Silent repo mutation during install is not required for the initial solution
- [x] Optional install hints are separated into follow-up work
close_reason: Design decision captured; explicit bootstrap chosen as the initial workflow

title: Design ai.package.local.yaml overlay semantics
description:
## Description
Define a first-class gitignored project-local manifest (`ai.package.local.yaml`) so developers can keep private project-specific additions without editing committed `ai.package.yaml`.

## Decision
The initial overlay model is intentionally simple and additive:
- `ai.package.local.yaml` is **opt-in only**
- aimgr will **read it when present**, but will **not auto-create it** and will **not auto-edit .gitignore**
- effective resources are the **union** of `ai.package.yaml` and `ai.package.local.yaml`, preserving base-manifest order and appending local-only additions
- exact duplicate resource refs are de-duplicated in the merged view
- effective `install.targets` are the **union** of base + local targets; explicit CLI `--target` still overrides manifest targets

## Command Behavior
The following commands must use the merged manifest view when the local overlay exists:
- `aimgr install` (zero-arg manifest install)
- `aimgr verify`
- `aimgr repair`
- `aimgr list`
- `aimgr uninstall` for manifest updates when `--no-save` is not used

Uninstall persistence rule:
- if a resource exists in either manifest and `aimgr uninstall` is allowed to save, remove it from every manifest file that declares it so the persisted state matches the user’s intent to remove it from the project

## Missing-Source Behavior
If `ai.package.local.yaml` references a resource that is unavailable in the user’s local repo, commands should surface the same kind of error/warning they would for a missing base-manifest resource. The overlay is not silently ignored.

## Persistence / Gitignore Expectations
- `ai.package.local.yaml` is intended to be gitignored by users/projects
- aimgr treats it as a local convention, not a mandatory project artifact
- documentation should explain the recommended gitignore setup

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Overlay merge rules are documented on the issue
- [x] Command behavior is defined for `install`, `repair`, `verify`, `list`, and `uninstall` persistence
- [x] Persistence and gitignore expectations are defined
- [x] Interaction with the central team baseline is defined
close_reason: Design decisions captured; implementation can proceed

title: Decide central shared setup-manifest workflow
description:
## Description
Decide how teams should publish and consume centrally maintained shared `ai.repo.yaml` manifests while still keeping one shared local aimgr repository per user.

## Decision
The preferred baseline workflow is:
- teams publish shared setup manifests from a **central setup repository**
- projects and READMEs point users to the appropriate **manifest URL**
- users and CI bootstrap explicitly with `aimgr repo apply-manifest <url> && aimgr repo sync && aimgr install`
- reproducibility can come from **pinning the manifest URL to a tag/ref**
- shared upstream sources should use **canonical source names** curated centrally
- project-local manifests are optional and reserved for projects that truly ship their own resources

## In Scope
- consumption model for central shared manifests
- naming and merge expectations for team reliability
- interaction with one shared local repo per user

## Explicitly Out of Scope
- detailed design of the remote setup repository layout
- exact folder structure or publishing process inside that remote repo

## Key Constraints
- `repo apply-manifest` must not allow duplicate canonical sources to accumulate under different names
- per-project variation should live primarily in `ai.package.yaml`, not in divergent names for the same upstream source
- the workflow must remain understandable for many projects sharing one local repo

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Central shared manifests are the chosen default bootstrap model
- [x] Canonical source naming is part of the design direction
- [x] Remote setup-repo design is explicitly deferred
close_reason: Design decision captured; remote setup-repo design intentionally deferred

title: Improve shared team setup manifests and local overlays for multi-project aimgr teams

title: Restore repo verify handling for packages with invalid resource references
description:
## Description
The new package schema validation for resource validation changed repository verification behavior: `make test` now fails in `TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageInvalidRef` because `repo verify` no longer reports packages whose resource references have invalid format.

`resource.LoadPackage()` now rejects invalid package resource references during load. `repo verify` currently relies on `ListPackages()` and `GetPackage()`, both of which use `LoadPackage()`. As a result, packages with invalid refs are skipped instead of being reported under "Packages with missing resource references".

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run `make test` in `/home/hans/dev/github/ai-config-manager`.
2. Observe failing integration test `TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageInvalidRef`.
3. Alternatively create a repo package with refs like `invalid-format` or `unknown-type/resource-name` and run `./aimgr repo verify`.

## Expected Behavior
- `repo verify` reports packages containing invalid or unresolved resource references.
- Existing repo verify integration tests pass.
- `resource validate` keeps strict package schema validation.

## Actual Behavior
- `repo verify` skips these packages because strict package loading fails first.
- The invalid package is omitted from verification output, causing test failure.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `make test` passes again.
- [ ] `TestCLIRepoVerifyPackageInvalidRef` passes and reports the invalid package refs.
- [ ] `resource validate` still performs package schema validation as designed.
- [ ] No new regressions are introduced in package loading or repo verification behavior.
close_reason: Restored repo verify handling for invalid package refs without regressing strict resource validate schema checks

title: resource validate machine-readable output is polluted by repo-probe warnings
description:
## Description
During acceptance review for `ai-config-manager-o98c.4`, machine-readable `aimgr resource validate` output was not automation-safe in repo-missing scenarios.

When `AIMGR_REPO_PATH` points to a missing directory, `resource validate --format=json` emits logger warnings before the JSON payload, which breaks JSON parsing.

## Reproduction
1. Build `./aimgr`
2. Create a valid source tree and manifest-backed package
3. Run with a missing repo path, for example:
   - `AIMGR_REPO_PATH=/tmp/missing ./aimgr resource validate --format=json --repo-manifest /tmp/ai.repo.yaml package/release-bundle`
   - `AIMGR_REPO_PATH=/tmp/missing ./aimgr resource validate --format=json skill/demo-skill`
4. Observe stdout/stderr contains warning lines before the JSON object:

```text
Warning: failed to initialize logger: repo path does not exist: /tmp/missing
{ ...json... }
```

## Expected
For `--format=json` and `--format=yaml`, output must remain machine-readable so automation can parse it reliably. Repo-context probing should not prepend warnings that corrupt the payload.

## Observed
- Manifest-backed package validation succeeded with exit code 0, but JSON output was prefixed by warning lines.
- Missing-context validation returned exit code 2, but JSON error output was also prefixed by warning lines.
- This violates the epic requirement that machine-readable output and exit codes support automation.

## Likely Root Cause
- `cmd/resource_validate.go` calls `NewManagerWithLogLevel()` in `detectLocalRepoRoot()` just to probe whether a local repo exists.
- `pkg/repo/manager.go` writes `Warning: failed to initialize logger...` to stderr when the configured repo path does not exist.
- CLI integration tests cover JSON output for `--source-root`, but do not currently cover manifest-backed JSON output or missing-context JSON output with a missing repo path.

## Files to Check
- `cmd/resource_validate.go`
- `cmd/root.go`
- `pkg/repo/manager.go`
- `test/resource_validate_test.go`
- `cmd/resource_validate_test.go`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr resource validate --format=json ...` emits parseable JSON with no warning prefix when repo probing falls back past a missing repo path.
- [ ] `aimgr resource validate --format=yaml ...` emits parseable YAML under the same conditions.
- [ ] Manifest-backed package validation remains successful without requiring a manually created local repo.
- [ ] Missing-context failures still return exit code 2 and machine-readable diagnostics without payload corruption.
- [ ] Regression coverage exists for CLI JSON/YAML output in manifest-backed and missing-context cases.
close_reason: Fixed repo probe to resolve path without logger init and added JSON/YAML regression coverage for missing-repo manifest and missing-context flows

title: Implement static validation for skill, agent, and command resources
description:
## Description
Add the `resource validate` command path and hook it up to existing standalone validators so creators can validate skills, agents, and commands directly from a path or canonical resource ID without requiring repository context.

## Instructions
1. Add a new top-level `resource` command group if needed and implement `resource validate` under `cmd/` according to the approved design.
2. Accept a single target that may be a canonical resource ID or a filesystem path.
3. Reuse or extend `pkg/resource.ValidateSkill`, `ValidateAgent`, and `ValidateCommand` for static validation.
4. Ensure command validation works for standalone creator files and does not require the target file to already live under a repository `commands/` directory.
5. Implement shared human-readable and machine-readable output / exit code behavior consistent with the approved design.
6. Ensure standalone validation does not touch the live repository.
7. Add unit and integration coverage for path-based validation, resource-ID-based validation, output formatting, and failure handling.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/ (new `resource` / `resource validate` command files)
- pkg/resource/ (shared validation helpers and output structures as needed)
- cmd/completion_helpers.go or related completion wiring if needed
- tests under `cmd/` or `test/` for CLI behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr resource validate <skill-path-or-id>` validates skills.
- [ ] `aimgr resource validate <agent-path-or-id>` validates agents.
- [ ] `aimgr resource validate <command-path-or-id>` validates commands.
- [ ] Standalone command-file validation works even when the file is outside a repository `commands/` directory.
- [ ] Human-readable and machine-readable output are supported.
- [ ] Validation returns non-zero on failures and zero on success.
- [ ] Tests cover path-based and resource-ID-based validation plus standalone command-file validation.
- [ ] Static validation does not mutate or depend on the live repository.
close_reason: Implemented aimgr resource validate for skill/agent/command static validation with path/ID resolution, output formats, exit codes, and safety-focused tests

title: Implement contextual package validation with isolated temp repository state
description:
## Description
Implement package support for `aimgr resource validate` so creators can validate both package schema and package references against a source tree or repo manifest without manually building a test repository and without risking mutation of their live repository.

## Instructions
1. Reuse existing package parsing logic from `pkg/resource` and extract/reuse package-reference validation logic from `pkg/repo` rather than duplicating repository verification behavior.
2. Implement package validation for both package paths and package resource IDs.
3. Add contextual validation inputs such as `--source-root` and `--repo-manifest`.
4. Build the minimal isolated validation context needed for package resolution, using temp directories, an in-memory index, or a temporary repository manager.
5. Resolve nested resource IDs exactly as sync/import would, so validation reflects real canonical names.
6. Report missing references with actionable diagnostics and canonical-ID suggestions when close matches exist.
7. Add unit, integration, and safety tests using `t.TempDir()`, `repo.NewManagerWithPath()`, and isolated repo paths to prove validation does not touch the live repository.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/ (package validation command path and flags)
- pkg/resource/package.go (package parsing/schema validation enhancements)
- pkg/repo/package.go or a new package validation service
- pkg/repomanifest/ or related loading code if manifest-backed context is used
- tests under `cmd/`, `pkg/`, or `test/` for temp-context validation behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Package validation works from a package path or package resource ID.
- [ ] Basic package schema validation is performed before contextual reference resolution.
- [ ] `--source-root` can supply the validation context for referenced resources.
- [ ] `--repo-manifest` can supply a multi-source validation context when needed.
- [ ] Missing refs include useful suggestions for likely intended canonical IDs.
- [ ] Package validation reuses extracted package-reference validation logic instead of duplicating `repo verify` behavior.
- [ ] Tests prove validation is isolated from the live repository and does not mutate it.
close_reason: Completed: implemented package validation for resource validate (path and canonical ID) with two-phase schema+context checks, reusable repo package-reference validator shared with repo verify, source-root/repo-manifest context support, nested canonical resolution, actionable missing-ref suggestions, and isolated unit/integration/safety tests

title: Design CLI and validation architecture for resource validate
description:
## Description
Define the command surface and validation model for a unified `aimgr resource validate` workflow that supports all resource types while cleanly separating static validation from contextual validation.

## Instructions
1. Review existing validation entry points in `pkg/resource`, `pkg/repo/package.go`, `cmd/repo_verify.go`, `cmd/repo_apply.go`, and related Cobra structure under `cmd/`.
2. Design the CLI UX for `aimgr resource validate <resource-id-or-path>` including command placement under `cmd/resource.go` / `cmd/resource_validate.go` and flag behavior for `--source-root`, `--repo-manifest`, and `--format`.
3. Specify resolution rules for filesystem paths versus canonical resource IDs, including behavior when a canonical ID is provided without repository context or explicit source context.
4. Define validation modes by resource type:
   - skill / agent / command: standalone static validation
   - package: package schema validation plus contextual reference validation
5. Define reusable architecture boundaries so existing `pkg/resource` validators are reused, package reference validation logic is extracted rather than duplicated from repository verification, and output structure aligns with existing validation error / output patterns.
6. Define safety and isolation rules for any temporary repository or index usage, and specify the required test strategy (unit, integration, and safety coverage) using `t.TempDir()` / `repo.NewManagerWithPath()` patterns.
7. Record the approved design in the issue body or notes so follow-on implementation tasks can proceed without further product decisions.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/ (new command entrypoint and validate subcommand to be added later; this task is design only)
- pkg/resource/ (existing validators to be reused/extended later)
- pkg/repo/ and/or a new validation package (design target only)
- docs/ (design implications to capture later)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Command syntax, flags, and resolution rules are fully specified.
- [ ] Behavior for canonical resource IDs without repo context is explicitly defined.
- [ ] Static vs contextual validation behavior is defined for skill, agent, command, and package resources, including package schema validation.
- [ ] Architecture placement and reuse boundaries are documented so `resource validate` does not duplicate `repo verify` integrity logic.
- [ ] Output format and machine-readable schema are specified.
- [ ] Safety constraints and required test strategy are documented.
- [ ] Follow-on implementation tasks can proceed without further product decisions.
close_reason: Completed: documented approved CLI/validation architecture, resolution rules, output schema, safety constraints, and test strategy in issue comment

title: Add resource-level validation for AI resource creators
description:
## Description
Add a first-class creator workflow for validating individual AI resources before they are synced or installed. The new workflow should center on `aimgr resource validate <resource-id-or-path>` and cover skills, agents, commands, and packages.

## Goals
- Give creators one validation entry point for all resource types.
- Support fast static validation for skills, agents, commands, and package schema.
- Support contextual package validation against a source tree or repo manifest without requiring manual temp-repo setup.
- Return actionable diagnostics, machine-readable output, and non-zero exit codes when validation fails.
- Keep validation isolated from the user's live/global repository.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr resource validate` exists and supports canonical resource IDs and local filesystem paths.
- [ ] Skill, agent, and command validation works without requiring repository context.
- [ ] Package validation can resolve references using `--source-root` and `--repo-manifest` style context.
- [ ] Missing package refs report actionable diagnostics, including likely canonical-ID suggestions for nested resources.
- [ ] Tests and docs cover creator workflows and safety constraints.
- [ ] Acceptance review confirms validation does not mutate the live repository.
notes: Reviewer review applied: added .5 -> .1 dependency, tightened design scope for ID/path resolution and output schema, expanded package task to cover schema validation and reusable package-ref validation, and moved docs to follow implementation to reduce drift.

title: Fix final workspace gosec findings before v3.5.0 tag
description:
## Description

title: Create GitHub release v3.5.0
description:
## Description

## Instructions
1. Commit the changelog update and release-blocking fixes with a conventional commit message.
2. Push `main` so the release commit is on the remote default branch.
3. Wait for the GitHub Actions build for that pushed `main` commit to pass before creating the release tag.
4. Create an annotated tag: `git tag -a v3.5.0 -m "Release v3.5.0"`.
5. Push the tag: `git push origin v3.5.0`.
6. Monitor the tag-triggered GitHub Actions release workflow with `gh run watch`.
7. Confirm the GitHub release exists with `gh release view v3.5.0`.

## Files to Modify
- CHANGELOG.md — already prepared before this task
- Git history / tags — create and push the release commit and annotated tag

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The pushed `main` commit passes GitHub Actions before the tag is created
- [ ] Annotated tag `v3.5.0` exists locally and remotely
- [ ] The release workflow completes successfully
- [ ] `gh release view v3.5.0` succeeds

title: Consolidate three separate normalizeURL implementations into one
description:
## Problem

There are currently three URL-normalization implementations used for related Git-source identity operations:
- `pkg/workspace/manager.go`
- `cmd/repo_prune.go`
- `pkg/repomanifest/sourceid.go`

They are not behaviorally identical and can normalize the same logical URL differently, especially around whitespace and combined trailing suffix forms like `.git/` and `/.git`.

## Why This Matters

The normalized value feeds into persisted identity/state in multiple places:
- workspace cache hashing and cache path selection
- workspace metadata URL storage/comparison
- prune referenced-vs-unreferenced matching
- URL-based source ID generation

A mismatch here is a correctness risk, not just duplication.

## Required Implementation Scope

1. Create one canonical shared `NormalizeURL()` helper in a neutral reusable package.
2. Replace all three current normalization implementations with that helper.
3. Keep path normalization separate; this issue is about Git URL normalization only.
4. Use the loop-based suffix stripping behavior as the canonical baseline:
   - trim whitespace
   - lowercase
   - repeatedly strip trailing `/`
   - repeatedly strip trailing `.git`
   - stop when stable

## Required Validation Scope

This task is not complete without tests proving we do not break behavior.

### Unit/package tests
Update/add tests in:
- `pkg/workspace/manager_test.go`
- `cmd/repo_prune_test.go`
- `pkg/repomanifest/sourceid_test.go`

Cover at least:
- plain URL
- uppercase URL
- trailing slash
- `.git`
- `.git/`
- `/.git`
- multiple trailing slashes
- surrounding whitespace

### Cross-package consistency tests
Add a focused test proving the same input URL yields consistent results across:
- workspace normalization/hash
- prune matching semantics
- source ID normalization for URL sources

### Compatibility / persistence tests
Add tests that simulate existing persisted state produced under the old normalization and verify the updated code behaves safely. At minimum cover persisted workspace metadata / cache hash usage and URL-based source identity assumptions.

### Integration / end-to-end tests
Add or extend an integration/e2e test using a tricky URL form (for example `.git/`) and verify:
- add/sync uses one stable identity
- prune does not misclassify the cache
- source tracking remains intact across subsequent operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A single shared canonical `NormalizeURL()` helper exists and replaces all three current implementations.
- [ ] Canonical behavior is documented and tested for edge cases including `.git/`, `/.git`, repeated suffix stripping, and surrounding whitespace.
- [ ] Workspace cache hashing, prune comparison, and URL-based source ID generation are proven consistent for the same logical URL.
- [ ] Tests cover compatibility/persistence concerns so existing repos are not silently broken by the normalization change.
- [ ] At least one integration/e2e path exercises a tricky URL form and verifies end-to-end correctness.
close_reason: Implemented canonical normalizeURL with compatibility and tests

title: Add concurrent-process tests and concurrency docs
description:
## Description
Add end-to-end and integration coverage for concurrent multi-process repo mutation and document the resulting concurrency guarantees and limitations.

## Instructions
1. Add subprocess-based tests that run the built/local CLI against temp repos to exercise real multi-process behavior rather than only in-process helpers.
2. Cover the highest-risk combinations identified in the concurrency audit: `init+init`, `sync+sync`, `add+remove`, `apply-manifest+add`, `drop+other mutation`, and workspace cache contention flows.
3. Assert both safety outcomes and user-facing behavior: no corrupted repo state, no lost manifest/source metadata entries, and clear failures or waiting behavior when contention occurs.
4. Make the concurrent tests deterministic: coordinate process start/hold/release points explicitly rather than relying on timing luck alone.
5. Update user/developer docs to describe the locking model, which operations serialize repo-wide, which serialize per cache, and any known limitations.
6. Remove or correct stale comments/docs that currently claim protections not actually implemented.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/repo_*_test.go — add or extend CLI/integration coverage
- pkg/workspace/*_test.go — add lock/contention coverage
- pkg/repo/*_test.go — add focused concurrency tests where useful
- docs/CODING.md — document safe testing expectations for concurrency work
- docs/TESTING.md — document concurrent test strategy
- any user-facing repo/workspace docs that describe mutation behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Automated tests exercise concurrent multi-process mutation with temp repos.
- [ ] Concurrency tests use deterministic coordination rather than sleep-only timing.
- [ ] Documentation accurately describes implemented concurrency guarantees.
- [ ] No code comments remain that claim locking behavior not present in code.
- [ ] `make test` passes.
close_reason: Implemented concurrent-process tests and concurrency docs

title: Protect workspace cache mutations with per-cache locking
description:
## Description
Make shared workspace cache mutation safe under concurrent processes by introducing per-cache locking and safe coordination for shared workspace metadata updates.

## Instructions
1. Implement per-cache cross-process locking keyed by cache hash/path using `<repo>/.workspace/locks/caches/<cache-hash>.lock`.
2. Update `GetOrClone`, `Update`, `Remove`, and any prune/removal flows to acquire the appropriate cache lock for the full cache mutation section.
3. Explicitly solve the shared `.workspace/.cache-metadata.json` race using the dedicated metadata lock at `<repo>/.workspace/locks/workspace-metadata.lock`. Per-cache locking alone is not sufficient because different cache operations still read-modify-write the same shared metadata file.
4. Follow the explicit lock ordering model: `repo lock -> cache lock -> workspace metadata lock`.
5. Hold the workspace metadata lock only for short shared-metadata read-modify-write sections. Do not hold it across clone/fetch/pull/network or long filesystem work.
6. Ensure internal workspace methods invoked from repo commands operate under the already-held repo lock and then acquire lower-level locks as needed; they must not try to acquire the repo lock themselves.
7. Protect workspace metadata writes with atomic writes and ensure metadata updates cannot lose entries under concurrent access.
8. Reconcile current code/comments that claim flock-based safety with the actual implementation.
9. Verify that clone/fetch/checkout/reset/stash/pull flows do not operate concurrently on the same cache worktree.
10. Ensure repo-level operations that enumerate then remove caches (`repo prune`) process caches one at a time rather than holding multiple cache locks concurrently.

## Files to Modify
- pkg/workspace/manager.go
- cmd/repo_prune.go
- docs/** or code comments that currently describe workspace locking guarantees
- pkg/** (create) — add workspace lock helper(s) if separate from repo lock implementation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Workspace cache mutation is serialized per cache across processes using `.workspace/locks/caches/<hash>.lock`.
- [ ] Shared workspace metadata updates are serialized with `.workspace/locks/workspace-metadata.lock` and do not lose updates when different caches change concurrently.
- [ ] Lock ordering between repo-wide and workspace/cache locks matches `repo -> cache -> workspace metadata` and is documented and verified.
- [ ] `.workspace/.cache-metadata.json` updates are atomic.
- [ ] `repo prune` cannot remove a cache that is actively being cloned/updated without safe coordination.
- [ ] Tests cover at least `sync+sync` on the same remote source and `prune+sync` / `prune+remote add` style interactions.
- [ ] `make test` passes.
close_reason: Implemented workspace cache and metadata locking

title: Protect repo mutators with repo-wide locking
description:
## Description
Apply repo-wide locking and atomic state updates across all repo mutating commands and hidden write paths so only one process can mutate a repository at a time.

## Instructions
1. Inventory and update every repo-wide mutating command/path to acquire the repo lock at `<repo>/.workspace/locks/repo.lock` for the full critical section: `repo init`, `repo add`, `repo sync`, `repo remove`, `repo drop`, `repo apply-manifest`, `repo repair`, deprecated `repo verify --fix`, and any other repo-level mutating entry points that touch shared repo state.
2. Ensure lower-level helper paths used by those commands do not bypass locking when invoked directly.
3. Update manifest and source metadata mutations to use the atomic write helper rather than plain load-modify-save writes.
4. Address hidden mutating paths, especially manifest migration during load, so they are either explicitly locked or refactored out of read-only flows.
5. Review interactions between repo-wide destructive operations (`drop`, `remove`) and in-flight mutation paths to ensure lock coverage is complete.
6. Include `repo prune` if the chosen locking model requires repo-wide coordination in addition to workspace/cache-level coordination.
7. Preserve existing user-facing behavior and error messages where practical, but return clear errors when lock acquisition fails.
8. Cover repo modification side effects beyond the primary files, including modification generation/cleanup paths that also mutate repo-managed state.
9. Ensure top-level repo commands are the outermost lock holders: they may call workspace/cache helpers that take lower-level locks, but no code path in this task may acquire the repo lock from inside an already-held cache or workspace-metadata lock.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/repo_init.go
- cmd/repo_add.go
- cmd/repo_sync.go
- cmd/repo_remove.go
- cmd/repo_drop.go
- cmd/repo_apply.go
- cmd/repo_repair.go
- cmd/repo_verify.go
- cmd/repo_prune.go — if repo-wide coordination is part of the final lock model
- pkg/repo/manager.go
- pkg/repo/add.go
- pkg/repo/remove.go
- pkg/repo/bulk.go
- pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go
- pkg/sourcemetadata/metadata.go
- pkg/modifications/generator.go

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All repo-wide mutating CLI commands acquire `<repo>/.workspace/locks/repo.lock` before changing repo state.
- [ ] Hidden write-on-load behavior is no longer an unlocked mutation path.
- [ ] Manifest/source metadata updates are atomic under concurrent access.
- [ ] Concurrent repo-wide mutation attempts fail safely or wait safely without corrupting repo state.
- [ ] Repo-level lock usage obeys the explicit ordering model and never reverses into lower-level-held sections.
- [ ] Targeted tests cover at least `init+init`, `add+add`, `add+remove`, `sync+sync`, `sync+add`, and `drop+other mutation` scenarios.
- [ ] `make test` passes.
close_reason: Implemented repo-wide locking across repo mutators

title: Make repo mutations safe under concurrent processes
description:
## Description
Make aimgr safe for concurrent multi-process repository mutation so simultaneous `repo init`, `repo add`, `repo sync`, `repo remove`, `repo drop`, `repo apply-manifest`, `repo prune`, and repair/fix flows do not corrupt repo state, lose manifest/metadata updates, or race on shared workspace caches.

## Goals
- Add explicit cross-process serialization for repo-wide mutations.
- Add explicit cross-process serialization for per-cache workspace mutations.
- Define a single lock ordering model so repo-wide and per-cache locks cannot deadlock.
- Replace plain non-atomic metadata/manifest writes with atomic file replacement.
- Eliminate or guard hidden write-on-load paths.
- Verify behavior with multi-process tests that exercise real CLI commands against temp repos.

## Lock Model (Explicit Design)
Use **OS-backed advisory file locks** on lock files stored under the repo's ignored workspace area. Do **not** use plain sentinel lock files.

### Lock file paths
- Repo-wide lock: `<repo>/.workspace/locks/repo.lock`
- Shared workspace metadata lock: `<repo>/.workspace/locks/workspace-metadata.lock`
- Per-cache lock: `<repo>/.workspace/locks/caches/<cache-hash>.lock`

### Lock primitive
- Use `flock`-style exclusive locking on an open file descriptor on supported POSIX platforms.
- The lock file path is just the anchor object; the actual lock is the OS lock on the open file handle.
- Crash safety requirement: if the process dies, the OS must release the lock automatically.

### Lock ordering
If an operation needs multiple locks, the order is always:
1. **repo lock**
2. **cache lock**
3. **workspace metadata lock**

Rules:
- Never acquire the repo lock while already holding a cache lock or metadata lock.
- Never acquire a cache lock while already holding the metadata lock.
- Hold the workspace metadata lock only for short read-modify-write sections on shared metadata; never hold it across clone/fetch/pull/network work.
- If multiple cache locks are ever needed, process caches one at a time rather than holding multiple cache locks concurrently.

### Operation-to-lock mapping
- `repo init`, `repo add`, `repo sync`, `repo remove`, `repo drop`, `repo apply-manifest`, `repo repair`, `repo verify --fix`: acquire the **repo lock** for the full repo mutation.
- Workspace cache clone/update/remove work inside those commands: while holding repo lock, acquire the relevant **cache lock** for the specific cache being mutated.
- Shared `.workspace/.cache-metadata.json` updates: while holding any required outer locks, acquire the **workspace metadata lock** only around metadata read-modify-write.
- `repo prune`: acquire the **repo lock** for the overall prune operation, then process candidate caches one at a time with the cache lock for each cache; use the workspace metadata lock only when reading/writing shared cache metadata.
- Internal workspace methods invoked outside top-level repo commands still follow the same lower-level rules: cache lock for cache mutation, metadata lock for shared workspace metadata mutation, and they must not try to acquire the repo lock from inside those lower-level sections.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All repo-mutating commands are protected by a repo-wide process lock or a documented narrower lock where appropriate.
- [ ] Workspace cache mutations (`GetOrClone`, `Update`, `Remove`, `Prune`) are serialized per cache and safe when invoked concurrently.
- [ ] A documented lock-ordering model prevents deadlocks between repo-wide locks, per-cache locks, and the shared workspace metadata lock.
- [ ] Manifest and metadata writes use atomic replacement rather than plain `os.WriteFile` load-modify-save races.
- [ ] Git commit/staging behavior no longer sweeps unrelated concurrent changes into the wrong commit.
- [ ] Hidden mutating paths (such as manifest migration on load) are either removed from read paths or protected by the same locking model.
- [ ] Automated tests cover concurrent mutation scenarios using temp repos and subprocesses.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Add repo locking and atomic write foundation
description:
## Description
Create the shared concurrency foundation for repo mutation safety: a repo-wide cross-process lock and atomic file write helper(s). This task establishes the primitives all mutating commands will use.

## Instructions
1. Add a reusable cross-process locking mechanism rooted at `<repo>/.workspace/locks/`.
2. Implement the explicit lock paths defined by the epic:
   - repo lock: `<repo>/.workspace/locks/repo.lock`
   - workspace metadata lock: `<repo>/.workspace/locks/workspace-metadata.lock`
   - per-cache lock: `<repo>/.workspace/locks/caches/<cache-hash>.lock`
3. Use an OS-backed locking approach with crash-safe semantics. Prefer `flock`-style behavior on supported POSIX platforms so locks are released automatically if the process dies; do not rely on a plain stale lock-file pattern without recovery.
4. Define the lock API contract explicitly:
   - default acquisition behavior for CLI commands (blocking with timeout)
   - timeout/cancellation behavior
   - the error returned when a lock cannot be acquired in time
   - whether a non-blocking/try-lock variant exists
5. Document and enforce that the repo lock is **not re-entrant**. Establish a clear convention for nested calls such as `Init()` from other locked operations so callers do not deadlock themselves.
6. Encode the lock ordering invariant in code comments and helper documentation: `repo lock -> cache lock -> workspace metadata lock`.
7. Clarify supported-platform expectations for the first implementation. Linux/POSIX support is required; Windows support may be deferred if needed, but the code structure should make that explicit rather than implicit.
8. Add an atomic write helper for repo-managed files that writes to a temp file in the same directory, fsyncs file contents, renames into place, and fsyncs the parent directory where supported.
9. Replace direct plain-file writes in shared metadata/manifest helpers where possible in this foundational layer, or introduce helpers that later tasks will wire in.
10. Ensure the lock directory can be bootstrapped before repo init completes; `repo init` must be able to create and use the lock path even in a brand-new repo.
11. Document locking guarantees and failure behavior in code comments so follow-up tasks can apply the primitives consistently.

## Files to Modify
- pkg/repo/manager.go — integrate shared lock entry points or manager plumbing
- pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go — prepare for atomic manifest writes
- pkg/metadata/metadata.go — prepare for atomic metadata writes
- pkg/sourcemetadata/metadata.go — prepare for atomic source metadata writes
- pkg/workspace/manager.go — avoid duplicating atomic-write helpers if shared code belongs here
- pkg/** (create) — add new lock and/or atomic write helper package(s) in the most appropriate existing location

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Lock helpers exist for repo, workspace metadata, and per-cache lock paths under `.workspace/locks/`.
- [ ] Lock semantics are documented: blocking behavior, timeout/cancellation, and lock-acquisition failure behavior.
- [ ] The design explicitly states the lock is non-reentrant and shows how nested callers avoid deadlock.
- [ ] Crash behavior is safe on supported platforms; a dead process does not leave the repo permanently wedged.
- [ ] The lock ordering rule `repo -> cache -> workspace metadata` is documented in code and helper comments.
- [ ] Platform support expectations for the first implementation are explicit in code/comments/tests.
- [ ] Atomic write helper exists and replaces or is ready to replace plain `os.WriteFile` for repo-managed state files.
- [ ] Helper behavior is covered by focused unit tests.
- [ ] No tests write to the real global repo; all tests use temp directories.
- [ ] `make unit-test` passes.
close_reason: Implemented locking and atomic write foundation

title: Fix repeated repo sync removals for nested command metadata
description:
## Description

`aimgr repo sync` incorrectly detects nested commands as removed every other run because pre-sync inventory uses the flattened metadata filename (`opencode-coder-status`) instead of the canonical resource name stored inside metadata (`opencode-coder/status`).

This makes repeated syncs oscillate between recreating metadata and removing it again for nested commands from sources like `open-coder`.

## Instructions

1. Update `cmd/repo_sync.go` so `collectResourcesBySource()` reads the canonical resource name from metadata content (`meta.Name` / `pkgMeta.Name`) and only falls back to the flattened filename when the stored name is empty.
2. Add focused regression coverage in `cmd/repo_sync_test.go` for nested command metadata:
   - verify pre-sync inventory keeps the nested command name from metadata
   - verify repeated `runSync()` calls do not mark the nested command as removed and leave metadata in place
3. Format affected Go files and run focused tests plus relevant package tests.

## Files to Modify

- `cmd/repo_sync.go` — fix inventory name source for nested resources
- `cmd/repo_sync_test.go` — add regression tests for nested command sync behavior

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Pre-sync inventory uses canonical nested command names from metadata
- [ ] Repeated sync does not remove metadata for nested commands that still exist in source
- [ ] Regression tests cover the nested command case
- [ ] `go test ./cmd` passes
close_reason: Fixed: collectResourcesBySource now uses canonical names from metadata content, preventing nested command sync ping-pong. Added regression tests covering inventory naming and repeated sync behavior for nested commands. Verified with go test ./cmd.

title: Fix CI golangci-lint failures blocking v3.4.0 release
description:
## Description
The v3.4.0 release quality-gates task is blocked by a failing GitHub Actions Build workflow on `main`. title: Migrate Go module path and install docs to v4
description:
## Description
Major release v4.0.0 requires the Go module path to move from `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3` to `.../v4`. Without this migration, `go install github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v4/cmd/aimgr@v4.0.0` will fail even if the GitHub release succeeds.

## Instructions
1. Update `go.mod` module path from `/v3` to `/v4`.
2. Update all internal Go imports that reference `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3/...`.
3. Update release/build metadata injection paths in `.goreleaser.yaml` and `.github/workflows/build.yml` from `/v3/pkg/version` to `/v4/pkg/version`.
4. Update user-facing install documentation from `/v3` to `/v4`, including the README, SECURITY.md, and ai-resource-manager skill docs that show `go install` commands.
5. Run `go mod tidy` after the import/module updates.
6. Do not tag or release as v4.0.0 until this migration is complete.

## Files to Modify
- go.mod (update module path)
- cmd/**/*.go (update internal imports)
- pkg/**/*.go (update internal imports)
- test/**/*.go (update internal imports)
- .goreleaser.yaml (update ldflags import path)
- .github/workflows/build.yml (update ldflags import path)
- README.md (update `go install` path)
- SECURITY.md (update `go install` path)
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/SKILL.md (update `go install` path)
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/install-uninstall.md (update `go install` path)
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/manage-repository.md (update `go install` path)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `go.mod` declares `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v4`
- [ ] Internal imports no longer reference `/v3`
- [ ] Release/build configs reference `/v4/pkg/version`
- [ ] User-facing install docs use `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v4/cmd/aimgr`
- [ ] `go mod tidy` completes successfully
- [ ] The repository builds and tests successfully in the later quality-gate task
