title: Acceptance Review: Owned-folder clean and repair semantics
description:
## Description
Verify the new owned-folder model for `aimgr clean`, `aimgr repair`, `aimgr verify`, and deprecated `aimgr verify --fix` before the epic is closed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr clean` removes files, symlinks, broken links, nested content, and stray directories from owned resource directories without prompts while leaving `ai.package.yaml` and non-resource tool config files untouched
- [ ] `aimgr clean` behaves as a no-op with informative output when no tool directories exist and emits the documented warning when the manifest is missing
- [ ] `aimgr clean --help` does not show `--yes`
- [ ] `aimgr repair` with a valid manifest restores declared resources, removes undeclared content, and reports planned actions correctly in `--dry-run`
- [ ] `aimgr repair` with missing or invalid `ai.package.yaml` fails without destructive filesystem changes
- [ ] `aimgr repair --help` does not show `--reset` or `--force`, and repair JSON output exposes the planned/applied installs-fixes-removals information needed by scripts
- [ ] `aimgr verify` and deprecated `aimgr verify --fix` align with the final repair/clean semantics, use `undeclared` terminology instead of the old project-drift wording, and do not reference removed flags in help/output
- [ ] Docs and AI resource manager guidance include the migration from `aimgr repair --reset --force` to `aimgr clean && aimgr repair`, explain `--prune-package`, and explain reinstall-on-repair behavior
- [ ] Quality gates and verification commands pass, including `make build` and `make test`

## Owner
verifier
close_reason: Verified: owned-folder clean/repair acceptance criteria passed via help/docs inspection, manual CLI scenarios, make build, and make test

title: Rework aimgr repair as manifest reconciliation
description:
## Description
Change `aimgr repair` from a mixed repair/reset flow into a manifest reconciliation command for owned resource directories while keeping safety, scripting support, and manifest-maintenance behavior coherent.

## Decisions for This Task
- Keep `--dry-run`; it is the required preview mechanism for destructive reconcile actions
- Keep `--prune-package` as an orthogonal manifest-cleanup feature; adapt it so it still behaves coherently alongside reconciliation
- Remove CLI handling and help text for `--reset` and `--force`
- Reuse the same owned-directory detection model as `clean` and `verify`
- Install or fix declared resources before bulk-deleting undeclared content so partial failures leave the project recoverable

## Instructions
1. Reuse or extract shared logic for determining owned resource directories so `repair`, `clean`, and `verify` operate on the same set of folders. Coordinate with the clean task by using the same helper/entry point for owned-directory detection.
2. Load and validate `ai.package.yaml` before destructive changes. If the manifest is missing or invalid, return an error and make no filesystem changes.
3. Expand package references into the concrete desired resources before reconciling filesystem state.
4. Compute planned installs, fixes/replacements, removals, and optional manifest-prune actions. `--dry-run` must report these planned actions in both table and JSON output without applying them.
5. For each declared resource path, replace conflicting/broken/wrong entries as needed and install or fix resources so all declared resources are healthy.
6. After declared resources are healthy, remove remaining undeclared files, symlinks, and directories from owned resource directories.
7. Preserve/adapt `--prune-package` as a separate manifest-cleanup phase that still validates manifest refs against the repo and works correctly with `--dry-run` and the new output model.
8. Update structured repair output so removals, installs/fixes, prune-package edits, and failures are clearly reported. If the JSON schema changes, treat it as a documented breaking change and coordinate with docs/tests.
9. Update tests for reconcile behavior, dry-run, package expansion, conflicting paths, output, and any repair-related expectations that would otherwise break before the verify task lands.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/repair.go` — replace unmanaged/reset handling with reconcile logic and updated flags/help text
- `cmd/repair_test.go` — rewrite unit coverage for manifest reconciliation behavior
- `test/repair_test.go` — update CLI/integration coverage for reconciliation flows
- `cmd/project_verify_test.go` — adjust any repair-related expectations that must stay coherent before the verify task completes
- Any shared helpers needed for owned-directory detection or reconcile planning

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] With a valid manifest, `aimgr repair` restores all declared resources and removes undeclared content from owned resource directories
- [ ] Missing or invalid `ai.package.yaml` fails before destructive cleanup starts and leaves filesystem state unchanged
- [ ] `--dry-run` previews planned deletions, installs, fixes/replacements, and manifest-prune changes in both table and JSON output without making changes
- [ ] `aimgr repair` no longer exposes `--reset` or `--force`, and `--prune-package` remains supported with coherent behavior
- [ ] Partial failures leave the project in a recoverable state instead of bulk-deleting unrelated content first
- [ ] Automated tests cover reconcile behavior, package expansion, conflicting paths, dry-run, and output/schema expectations
close_reason: Implemented manifest reconciliation for repair with dry-run/prune-package planning and updated tests

title: Adopt owned-folder semantics for clean and repair
description:
## Description
Adopt a manifest-driven ownership model for project resource directories. aimgr should treat supported commands, skills, and agents folders as owned state instead of distinguishing managed, unmanaged, and orphaned entries.

## Decisions
- aimgr owns the contents of supported project commands, skills, and agents directories for detected tools
- `aimgr clean` deletes all content inside owned resource directories with no prompt and does not edit `ai.package.yaml`
- `aimgr repair` reconciles owned resource directories to `ai.package.yaml`: validate the manifest first, install or fix declared resources, and remove undeclared content
- `aimgr repair` keeps `--dry-run` as the safety preview mechanism and keeps `--prune-package` as an orthogonal manifest-cleanup feature
- Remove `aimgr repair --reset`, `aimgr repair --force`, and `aimgr clean --yes`
- `aimgr clean && aimgr repair` replaces the old `aimgr repair --reset --force` workflow
- `aimgr repair` must fail safely when `ai.package.yaml` is missing or invalid instead of silently wiping owned directories
- `aimgr repair` should leave the project in a recoverable state on partial failure by restoring declared resources before bulk deletion of unrelated undeclared content
- `aimgr repair` will reinstall declared resources that were manually removed; docs must explain that permanent removal requires updating `ai.package.yaml`

## Goals
- Make cleanup and repair behavior predictable from the manifest
- Remove the user-facing managed/unmanaged/orphaned mental model for project repairs
- Keep related command help, structured output, tests, and docs aligned with the new semantics
- Preserve compatibility for existing scripting and manifest-maintenance workflows where practical

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Repair reconciles owned resource directories to the manifest and supports dry-run preview
- [ ] Clean empties owned resource directories without confirmation
- [ ] Verify/help/output no longer reference removed flags or outdated terminology for project drift
- [ ] Documentation and AI resource manager references describe the new ownership model, migration path, and reinstall behavior accurately
- [ ] Acceptance review passes after automated tests and manual verification

## Out of Scope
- Repo-level metadata repair behavior (`aimgr repo repair`)
- Redesigning package/repository management beyond keeping `repair --prune-package` coherent with the new project-folder ownership model
close_reason: Completed owned-folder clean/repair implementation, docs, and acceptance review

title: Align aimgr verify and regression coverage with owned-folder semantics
description:
## Description
Update `aimgr verify`, its regression coverage, and its deprecated `--fix` behavior so reported issues and repair guidance match the new owned-folder cleanup and manifest reconciliation model.

## Decisions for This Task
- Replace managed/unmanaged/orphaned user-facing language for project drift with manifest-driven terminology
- Keep deprecated `aimgr verify --fix` working as a warning-emitting wrapper around the new repair behavior until a separate removal decision is made
- Reuse the same owned-directory detection model as `repair` and `clean`
- Rename the old `orphaned` issue classification to `undeclared`; keep `broken`, `wrong-repo`, `not-installed`, and `unreadable` unless code changes require a clearly documented consolidation

## Instructions
1. Replace or adapt issue classifications/messages for extra content so verify clearly describes content that exists in owned directories but is not declared in `ai.package.yaml`.
2. Ensure verify guidance never references removed flags and accurately explains the revised `repair`, `clean`, and `verify --fix` behavior.
3. Update structured output expectations consistently in code, tests, and docs. Use `undeclared` terminology anywhere the old `orphaned` classification or wording appeared.
4. Update the deprecated `verify --fix` path so it warns and follows the new repair semantics instead of preserving the old reset/unmanaged logic.
5. Add or update unit/integration coverage for undeclared content, missing resources, broken resources, wrong-repo resources, output/help text, and verify/repair interplay.
6. Coordinate with the repair task on any shared helpers or minimal temporary test fixes so the branch remains coherent.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/project_verify.go` — align issue detection, descriptions, `--fix` behavior, and repair suggestions with the owned-folder model
- `cmd/project_verify_test.go` — update output/help/behavior expectations
- Relevant project verify/repair integration tests under `test/` as needed
- Any shared helper touched to keep owned-directory logic consistent

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Verify output and suggestions match the final `repair`, `clean`, and deprecated `verify --fix` semantics
- [ ] No verify output, help text, or tests reference managed/unmanaged cleanup or removed flags
- [ ] `undeclared` terminology is used consistently across table output, JSON output, tests, and docs where the old `orphaned` wording existed
- [ ] Extra content, missing resources, broken resources, wrong-repo resources, and verify/repair interplay are covered by automated tests
- [ ] JSON and table output remain predictable for scripts and users
close_reason: Aligned verify terminology/behavior with owned-directory reconciliation and added regression coverage

title: Make aimgr clean wipe owned resource directories without prompts
description:
## Description
Simplify `aimgr clean` so it unconditionally removes everything inside aimgr-owned commands, skills, and agents directories for detected tools.

## Instructions
1. Remove the preview/confirmation flow and delete the `--yes` flag.
2. Reuse the same owned-directory detection model as `repair` and `verify`; extract or reuse a shared helper so all three commands operate on the same owned directories and cleanup boundaries stay consistent.
3. Delete all contents inside owned resource directories, including repo symlinks, broken symlinks, wrong-repo symlinks, regular files, nested namespace content, and stray subdirectories, while leaving the owned root directories themselves in place.
4. Leave non-resource tool config files alone and do not modify `ai.package.yaml`.
5. If no tool directories are detected, exit successfully with an informative no-op message.
6. If `ai.package.yaml` is missing, emit a non-blocking warning that `aimgr repair` will not be able to restore resources.
7. Add structured output for clean so table/text and JSON output can report removed entries and counts for scripting and review.
8. Update command help and examples to describe unconditional cleanup.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/clean.go` — remove interactive behavior, add structured output, and implement full directory-content cleanup
- `cmd/clean_test.go` — cover unconditional wipe semantics, no-op/warning cases, structured output, and untouched manifest/config behavior
- Any shared cleanup helpers needed by the new implementation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr clean` removes every entry inside owned commands/skills/agents directories and leaves those root directories empty
- [ ] `aimgr clean` no longer prompts and no longer exposes `--yes`
- [ ] `ai.package.yaml` remains unchanged after `clean`
- [ ] Non-resource tool files outside owned resource directories remain untouched
- [ ] `aimgr clean` is a no-op with informative output when no tool directories are detected
- [ ] Table/text and JSON output report removed entries with enough detail for scripting/review and report counts consistently
close_reason: Implemented clean owned-directory wipe semantics, removed --yes/prompt flow, added table/json structured output, warnings, and regression coverage.

title: Release v3.3.0
description:
## Description
Prepare and publish the v3.3.0 release from main using the tag-triggered GitHub Actions release workflow.

## Release Rationale
- Latest published tag is v3.2.0.
- Commits since v3.2.0 include `feat(copilot): support GitHub Copilot agents`, so the next semantic version bump is minor.

## Scope
1. Run required quality gates (`make test`, `make build`, CI check, clean tree).
2. Add a `3.3.0` entry to `CHANGELOG.md` summarizing user-visible changes.
3. Commit and push the changelog update to `main`.
4. Create and push annotated tag `v3.3.0` to trigger `.github/workflows/release.yml`.
5. Enhance GitHub release notes after GoReleaser publishes artifacts.
6. Verify release assets and installation from the published tag.
7. Close release tasks and the epic after verification.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] GitHub release `v3.3.0` has curated release notes
- [ ] Published install verification succeeds (`go install ...@v3.3.0`)
- [ ] All child release tasks are closed
notes: Version selected: v3.3.0 because commits since v3.2.0 include feat(copilot): support GitHub Copilot agents.
close_reason: Release v3.3.0 completed

title: Copilot target falsely reports command install success
description:
## Summary
During acceptance review for ai-config-manager-4a5.4, `aimgr install command/<name> --target copilot` reported success and added the command to `ai.package.yaml`, but it did not install any artifact for the Copilot target.

## Reproduction
1. Initialize a temporary aimgr repo with a command resource such as `commands/test-cmd.md`
2. Run `./aimgr install command/test-cmd --target copilot --project-path <tmp-project>`
3. Observe stdout reports `Installed command 'test-cmd'`
4. Run `./aimgr list --path <tmp-project>` or inspect the project directory

## Expected
Because Copilot prompt/command installs are intentionally unsupported, the command should fail clearly or be rejected for the Copilot target. It must not claim success or add a manifest entry for something that was not installed.

## Observed
- No `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md` or other Copilot command artifact was created
- No Copilot tool directory was created
- `ai.package.yaml` was updated with `command/test-cmd`
- `aimgr list` and `aimgr verify` then reported no tool directories found

## Why this matters
This violates the documented contract that Copilot command installs remain unsupported and creates a false in-sync state for users.

## Suggested follow-up
- Reject unsupported Copilot command installs explicitly
- Prevent manifest updates when nothing was installed
- Add regression coverage for unsupported command installs against the Copilot target
close_reason: Implemented explicit copilot command install rejection; prevented false success/manifest update; added regression tests

title: Design Copilot agent-only mapping strategy
description:
## Description
Design the agent-only support model for the GitHub Copilot / VSCode target in aimgr. The user explicitly chose not to support Copilot prompt/slash-command files. The remaining design problem is how to map aimgr's logical `agent` resource to Copilot's validated custom-agent convention: `.github/agents/*.agent.md`.

## Design Decision
Aimgr should support Copilot custom agents by keeping a shared logical `agent` resource model and adding tool-specific output naming rules for installation and generated modifications.

### Chosen Direction
1. **No Copilot prompt support**
   - Do not add Copilot command support.
   - Do not map existing `command` resources to `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md`.
   - Keep the current copilot target command support disabled and document that limitation.

2. **Shared logical agent resource**
   - Continue treating repository agents as the existing logical `agent` resource.
   - Preserve current repository authoring/discovery format (`agents/*.md`) to avoid introducing a Copilot-only source format in the repo.
   - Accept `.agent.md` as an install/render target concern, not a new repository resource type.

3. **Tool-specific output naming**
   - Introduce a tool-aware path/naming rule for installed agents.
   - Claude/OpenCode continue to install as `<name>.md`.
   - Copilot installs as `.github/agents/<name>.agent.md`.
   - This output rule must also apply to `.modifications/` generated variants so mapped agent files can be installed consistently.

4. **Conservative file-content strategy**
   - Reuse the existing agent frontmatter/content model initially.
   - Do not invent a Copilot-specific frontmatter transform unless validated documentation requires one.
   - If later testing shows field-level incompatibilities, address them as explicit follow-up work rather than silently transforming agent semantics now.

5. **Detection and discovery**
   - Project tool detection for Copilot should recognize `.github/agents` in addition to `.github/skills` once agent support is implemented.
   - Repository discovery/import can remain centered on logical `agent` resources and should not require repo authors to store source files as `.agent.md`.
   - If importing from an existing Copilot project folder becomes important later, loader logic may normalize `.agent.md` filenames back to logical agent names.

## Design Implications
- The current code assumes agent install and modification files always end in `.md`; this must become tool-aware.
- `ToolInfo` is currently insufficient to fully express filename conventions. Either extend it with agent filename metadata or introduce a small resource-target path helper owned by installer/modifications code.
- The resource model should continue to separate **logical resource identity** (`agent/foo`) from **tool-specific installed artifact names** (`foo.md` vs `foo.agent.md`).

## Recommended Implementation Shape
- Add a shared helper for target artifact paths/names by `(resource type, tool, resource name)`.
- Use that helper in installer symlink creation, uninstall/list logic, and modification path generation.
- Enable Copilot agent support only after all code paths use the shared helper.
- Keep Copilot command support disabled and documented.

## Follow-through for Existing Tasks
- `ai-config-manager-4a5.2` should proceed with the agent-only implementation based on this design.
- `ai-config-manager-4a5.3` should be closed as out of scope because Copilot prompt/command support was explicitly rejected.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Copilot prompt support is explicitly rejected and documented
- [ ] Copilot agent support uses the existing logical agent resource model
- [ ] Tool-specific output naming strategy is defined for installer and modifications paths
- [ ] No implicit `command -> prompt` mapping is introduced
- [ ] Downstream implementation task has a clear contract to build against
close_reason: Agent-only mapping strategy chosen: keep shared logical agent resources, no Copilot prompt support, and use tool-specific output naming with .agent.md for Copilot installs.

title: Support GitHub Copilot agents in aimgr
description:
## Description
Extend aimgr's GitHub Copilot / VSCode target beyond the current skills-only model so repositories can manage supported custom agents through the same install, list, uninstall, repair, and documentation workflows used for other tools.

This work is grounded in current GitHub Copilot behavior. The validated upstream convention for custom agents is `.github/agents/*.agent.md`.

## Goals
- Copilot tool metadata and detection reflect supported agent directories
- aimgr install/list/uninstall/repair workflows work for Copilot agents
- tests and docs cover supported behavior and remaining limitations

## Non-Goals
- Copilot prompt/slash-command support
- Copilot marketplace compatibility work
- hooks, utils, MCP/LSP plugin extras

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All child tasks completed
- [ ] Acceptance Review task passed
close_reason: Completed Copilot agent support: validated conventions, implemented agent-only support, explicitly rejected unsupported command installs, and passed acceptance review ai-config-manager-4a5.4.

title: Validate GitHub Copilot agent and command conventions
description:
## Description
Confirm the current GitHub Copilot / VSCode support model for custom agents and commands before aimgr changes behavior. aimgr currently treats the copilot/vscode target as skills-only. We need a verified source of truth for project-level directories, whether direct installation works outside plugin packaging, and any limitations that should remain documented.

## Validated Upstream Conventions (March 2026)
- VS Code project skills: `.github/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`
- VS Code custom agents: `.github/agents/*.agent.md`
- VS Code slash commands: `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md` (prompt files, not `.github/commands`)
- Copilot CLI custom agents: `.github/agents/*.agent.md` and `~/.copilot/agents`
- Copilot CLI instructions: `.github/copilot-instructions.md`, `.github/instructions/**/*.instructions.md`, `AGENTS.md`
- Copilot CLI plugins can also ship `commands`, `agents`, `skills`, hooks, and MCP config via `plugin.json`, but that packaging model diverges from direct workspace installation

## Instructions
1. Review current GitHub Copilot documentation/reference for project, user, and plugin locations for agents and commands.
2. Verify whether aimgr should install to `.github/agents`, `.github/prompts`, or another convention, and call out any divergence between plugin packaging and project-level configuration.
3. Record the chosen conventions and constraints in relevant code comments and contributor docs so later tasks have a stable contract.
4. If direct command installation is not actually supported by aimgr's current command resource model, do not invent support; document the limitation and create a discovered-from follow-up issue.

## Current Conclusion
- Copilot agent support is real upstream and uses `.github/agents/*.agent.md`.
- VS Code slash commands are prompt files in `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md`, not `commands/*.md`.
- Copilot CLI supports slash commands and plugin-defined commands, but not through the same direct project-level file contract aimgr currently uses for Claude/OpenCode commands.
- Therefore aimgr should remain conservative for now: keep direct Copilot skill installs, document the agent/prompt-file conventions, and avoid claiming generic command support until there is an explicit mapping or resource model.

## Files to Modify
- README.md — update support matrix/notes if needed
- docs/reference/supported-tools.md — record validated Copilot conventions and current aimgr contract
- pkg/tools/types.go — update Copilot comments only if conventions are verified
- pkg/install/copilot_workflow_integration_test.go — keep a guard/documentation test for the current contract

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Current Copilot agent/command conventions are documented from authoritative sources
- [ ] aimgr has an explicit documented contract for Copilot agents and commands
- [ ] Unsupported direct-install behavior is called out instead of being silently assumed
close_reason: Validated authoritative Copilot conventions, documented current aimgr contract, and filed follow-up issue ai-config-manager-m7j for prompt-file/agent mapping strategy.

title: Add GitHub Copilot agent support
description:
## Description
Implement GitHub Copilot agent support in aimgr using the approved agent-only mapping design. The validated upstream convention is `.github/agents/*.agent.md`. Aimgr should keep its shared logical `agent` resource model and add tool-specific output naming so Copilot installs render agents as `.agent.md` files without introducing Copilot prompt support.

## Instructions
1. **Enable Copilot agent target metadata**
   - Update `pkg/tools/types.go` so Copilot advertises agent support.
   - Set the Copilot agents directory to `.github/agents`.
   - Update existing-tool detection so Copilot is detected when either `.github/skills` or `.github/agents` exists.
   - Ensure Copilot is detected exactly once when both directories exist.
   - Keep Copilot command support disabled.

2. **Introduce shared bidirectional agent artifact naming**
   - Add a single helper used by install/list/uninstall/verify/repair/modification flows to derive agent artifact names by tool.
   - The helper contract must be bidirectional:
     - logical agent name + tool -> installed filename
     - installed filename + tool -> logical agent name
   - Preserve existing behavior for Claude/OpenCode (`<name>.md`).
   - Define Copilot agent artifact names as `<name>.agent.md`.
   - Avoid scattering Copilot-specific suffix logic across multiple call sites.

3. **Apply naming helper to installer and removal flows**
   - Update agent installation in `pkg/install/installer.go` to symlink Copilot agents into `.github/agents/<name>.agent.md`.
   - Ensure list/uninstall/repair paths use the same naming rule so Copilot agents round-trip correctly.
   - Audit `cmd/uninstall.go` and related list/remove code paths for hardcoded `.md` assumptions.
   - Do not change command installation logic beyond preserving Copilot command-disable behavior.

4. **Apply naming helper to verification and repair flows**
   - Audit `cmd/project_verify.go` for agent filename assumptions.
   - Ensure tool-aware reverse name derivation is used anywhere installed files are matched back to logical resource names.
   - In particular, cover orphan detection and verify-directory logic so `.agent.md` files are interpreted correctly for Copilot.
   - Audit `cmd/repair.go` for any hardcoded agent filename/path assumptions and align it with the shared helper.

5. **Apply naming helper to generated modifications**
   - Update `pkg/modifications/generator.go` so tool-specific agent variants use the same tool-aware output naming.
   - Cover all affected functions, including modification path generation, lookup, and cleanup.
   - Copilot agent modifications should be generated with the `.agent.md` suffix.
   - Preserve existing Claude/OpenCode modification paths.

6. **Keep repository agent model unchanged**
   - Do not require repository source agents to be renamed to `.agent.md`.
   - Continue loading repository agents from logical `agents/*.md` source files.
   - If any helper logic touches resource naming, preserve logical resource identity as `agent/<name>` independent of installed filename.

7. **Document the final contract**
   - Update docs/help text to say Copilot supports skills and agents, but not prompt/command installs.
   - Explicitly note that Copilot agents are installed as `.github/agents/*.agent.md` while repository source agents remain normal aimgr `agent` resources.

8. **Add regression coverage**
   - Add/update tests covering:
     - Copilot tool metadata and detection
     - Copilot detection only once when both `.github/skills` and `.github/agents` exist
     - forward and reverse Copilot agent name mapping
     - Copilot agent install path naming
     - list/uninstall behavior for Copilot agents
     - verification/orphan-detection behavior for Copilot agents
     - modification path generation for Copilot agents
     - Copilot commands remaining unsupported
   - Run the relevant test suite for install/tools/resource/cmd verify behavior.

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/tools/types.go` — enable Copilot agent support and detection
- `pkg/tools/types_test.go` — update metadata/detection expectations
- `pkg/install/installer.go` — use shared tool-aware agent naming for install flows
- `pkg/install/agent_integration_test.go` — add Copilot agent install/list/uninstall coverage
- `pkg/install/copilot_workflow_integration_test.go` — add/update Copilot end-to-end coverage
- `pkg/modifications/generator.go` — use tool-aware agent modification naming
- `pkg/modifications/generator_test.go` — cover Copilot `.agent.md` modification paths
- `cmd/project_verify.go` — make agent verification/orphan handling tool-aware
- `cmd/project_verify_test.go` — add Copilot `.agent.md` verification coverage
- `cmd/uninstall.go` — make uninstall name handling tool-aware for Copilot agents
- `cmd/uninstall_test.go` — add Copilot uninstall coverage if applicable
- `cmd/repair.go` — audit and update any hardcoded agent artifact assumptions
- `README.md` — update support matrix/notes
- `docs/reference/supported-tools.md` — document Copilot agent-only contract
- `docs/user-guide/configuration.md` and/or relevant CLI help text — keep user-facing support statements accurate

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Copilot ToolInfo exposes agent support with `.github/agents`
- [ ] Copilot detection works when `.github/agents` exists even if `.github/skills` does not
- [ ] Copilot is detected exactly once when both `.github/skills` and `.github/agents` exist
- [ ] Shared helper supports both forward and reverse Copilot agent filename mapping
- [ ] Copilot agent installs create `.github/agents/<name>.agent.md`
- [ ] List/uninstall/repair/verify flows use the same Copilot agent naming rule
- [ ] Copilot agent modifications use `.agent.md` output names
- [ ] Repository source agents remain standard logical `agents/*.md` resources
- [ ] Copilot prompt/command support remains disabled and documented
- [ ] Relevant tests pass
notes:
Validated upstream agent location is .github/agents/*.agent.md, but current aimgr agent resources are plain .md files. Do not implement Copilot agent installation until the mapping/file-format strategy from ai-config-manager-m7j is resolved.
Design resolved in ai-config-manager-m7j: implement Copilot agents only. Keep repository agent resources as logical agents and render/install Copilot targets as .github/agents/<name>.agent.md via tool-specific output naming.
User requested reviewer pass on the agent-only Copilot implementation checklist before execution. Review should focus on whether the task contract is complete, safe, and consistent with the chosen no-prompts design.
Reviewer feedback applied: checklist now explicitly requires bidirectional filename mapping, covers cmd/project_verify.go and cmd/uninstall.go, and adds duplicate-detection safeguards for .github/skills + .github/agents.
close_reason: Implemented Copilot agent support with shared bidirectional agent artifact naming across install/list/uninstall/verify/repair/modifications; updated docs and regression tests.

title: Add GitHub Copilot command support
description:
## Description
Add GitHub Copilot command support to aimgr using the validated command convention from the research task. aimgr already manages command resources for Claude and OpenCode; this task should extend or safely limit Copilot support based on the verified runtime contract.

## Instructions
1. Use the validated Copilot command location/behavior from the prerequisite task.
2. Update ToolInfo and project detection for Copilot commands if direct project command installs are supported.
3. Ensure installer, list, uninstall, and repair flows handle Copilot commands consistently with other tools.
4. If Copilot only supports commands via plugin packaging and not project-level install, do not fake support; update docs/tests to reflect the limitation and create a discovered-from follow-up issue instead.
5. Add or update tests for the supported behavior and docs.

## Files to Modify
- pkg/tools/types.go
- pkg/tools/types_test.go
- pkg/install/installer.go
- pkg/install/installer_test.go
- pkg/install/copilot_workflow_integration_test.go
- README.md

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Copilot command support matches validated runtime behavior
- [ ] Supported installs create expected command symlinks or documented equivalents
- [ ] Unsupported direct-install behavior is explicitly documented instead of silently claimed
- [ ] List/uninstall/test coverage matches final behavior

notes: Validated command conventions: VS Code slash commands are prompt files in .github/prompts/*.prompt.md, not .github/commands. Copilot CLI supports slash commands/reusable prompts and plugin-defined commands, which diverge from the VS Code prompt-file model. Do not treat generic commands/*.md as directly installable to the copilot/vscode target without an explicit mapping/transform story.

title: Fix repair handling for package manifest entries
description:
## Description
`aimgr repair` fails when `ai.package.yaml` contains `package/...` entries that are marked `not-installed`.

The manifest validator accepts `package/...` references and `aimgr install package/<name>` is implemented, but the repair path still parses not-installed resources with `resource.ParseResourceReference()`, which rejects `package` and returns `invalid resource type: "package" (expected command/skill/agent)`.

This makes `aimgr repair` inconsistent with the manifest and install workflows.

## Instructions
1. Update the repair path for `not-installed` manifest issues so `package/<name>` entries are handled explicitly instead of going through the command/skill/agent-only parser.
2. Reuse the existing package installation behavior so repair installs package members the same way `aimgr install package/<name>` does.
3. Keep current error handling for missing packages and partial package failures.
4. Add or update tests covering package-based repair behavior.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/repair.go — handle package references during repair
- cmd/repair_test.go — add/update unit tests for package repair
- test/repair_test.go — verify CLI/integration behavior if needed

title: Implement manifest file loading for repo apply
description:
## Description
Implement manifest input loading for `repo apply`, restricted to explicit manifest files: local `ai.repo.yaml` paths and HTTP(S) URLs pointing directly to `ai.repo.yaml`.

## Instructions
1. Add loader logic that accepts:
   - a local file path to `ai.repo.yaml`
   - an HTTP(S) URL pointing to `ai.repo.yaml`
2. Validate that remote inputs are explicit manifest files rather than bare repository URLs.
3. Parse the remote or local YAML into the existing manifest model while preserving backward compatibility.
4. Define and implement the v1 rule for source resolution:
   - local-file apply may resolve relative `path:` values relative to the manifest directory
   - remote manifest apply must either reject ambiguous relative `path:` values with a clear error or transform them according to the approved spec
5. Add focused tests for valid local files, valid remote files, invalid non-file URLs, invalid YAML, and path-resolution behavior.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/repo_apply.go or shared manifest loader utilities
- pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go or new loader package
- cmd/ and test/ repo apply loader tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Local manifest files load successfully.
- [ ] Remote HTTP(S) manifest files load successfully.
- [ ] Bare/non-file remote URLs are rejected with a clear error.
- [ ] Invalid YAML produces a clear parse error.
- [ ] Local relative `path:` values resolve as specified.
- [ ] Remote relative `path:` handling matches the approved v1 spec and is tested.
close_reason: Implemented manifest apply loader for explicit local/remote ai.repo.yaml inputs with path resolution/rejection rules and focused tests

title: Shareable repo manifests via repo apply
description:
## Description
Add a shareable manifest workflow so users can bootstrap aimgr from a committed `ai.repo.yaml` by applying a local manifest file or a remote manifest file URL.

## Goals
- Provide a simple bootstrap flow for `aimgr repo apply <manifest-file>`.
- Support exactly two v1 inputs: a local `ai.repo.yaml` path and an HTTP(S) URL pointing to `ai.repo.yaml`.
- Keep existing per-source `include` filter syntax intact.
- Keep `ai.repo.yaml` portable and human-authored rather than filled with local-only state.
- Merge applied manifests into existing local config predictably and idempotently.

## Out of Scope
- Bare repository URL inputs such as `aimgr repo apply https://github.com/org/repo`.
- Lockfiles and version pinning beyond current capabilities.
- New resource filter syntax (reuse current `source.include` patterns).
- Repo-relative remote `path:` sources unless they can be represented without ambiguous filesystem semantics; v1 should prefer `url:` sources for remotely shared manifests.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] CLI responsibilities for `repo init` and `repo apply` are documented.
- [ ] External/shareable manifest format is defined and keeps current filter support.
- [ ] Local-only source IDs/state are not required in the shareable manifest.
- [ ] `aimgr repo apply <manifest-file>` works for a local file and for a remote `ai.repo.yaml` URL.
- [ ] Merge/conflict behavior is explicit, tested, and documented.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Specify repo apply CLI and shareable manifest format
description:
## Description
Define the first-pass UX and schema for sharing an aimgr repository configuration through `ai.repo.yaml`, with `aimgr repo apply <manifest-file>` accepting only explicit manifest file inputs.

## Instructions
1. Review the current repo command surface in `cmd/repo_init.go`, `cmd/repo_add.go`, `cmd/repo_sync.go`, and the manifest model in `pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go`.
2. Write down the command responsibilities for:
   - `aimgr repo init` (local repository bootstrap only)
   - `aimgr repo apply <manifest-file>` (load and merge a local file path or HTTP(S) URL to `ai.repo.yaml`)
   - any explicitly deferred future command such as export/lockfile handling
3. Define the shareable `ai.repo.yaml` schema, preserving the current `source.include` filter syntax and examples.
4. Define merge rules for repeated apply, duplicate names, same-source reapply, and conflicting source definitions.
5. Define how relative `path` entries inside an applied manifest are resolved, including the v1 rule for remote manifests.
6. Record the decision that internal/generated source IDs are local-only unless a different approach is explicitly approved.
7. Produce user-facing CLI/doc examples that show concrete-file apply only, such as `aimgr repo apply ./ai.repo.yaml` and `aimgr repo apply https://example.com/ai.repo.yaml`.

## Files to Modify
- docs/user-guide/sources.md — document the shared manifest workflow and examples
- docs/reference/pattern-matching.md — document that shared manifests keep current include/filter syntax
- docs/internals/repository-layout.md or a new internal design doc — record CLI and merge rules
- cmd/repo_init.go and planned `cmd/repo_apply.go` help text notes — align on the spec wording

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The command contract for `repo init` vs `repo apply` is documented.
- [ ] The spec explicitly limits v1 apply inputs to local manifest files and HTTP(S) manifest file URLs.
- [ ] The external manifest example includes `include` filters using the existing pattern syntax.
- [ ] Merge/conflict rules are documented for additive merge, duplicate names, and re-apply behavior.
- [ ] Relative path resolution rules are documented, including the v1 behavior for remote manifests.
- [ ] The shareable manifest explicitly excludes local-only/generated IDs.
close_reason: Documented repo apply v1 contract, shareable manifest schema, merge/conflict rules, and path-resolution behavior with concrete examples

title: Refactor manifest persistence for shareable configs
description:
## Description
Adjust manifest loading and saving so `ai.repo.yaml` can serve as a portable shared config without depending on persisted generated source IDs or other local-only state.

## Instructions
1. Update `pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go` and its callers so existing manifests with `id` fields still load correctly.
2. Ensure future saved/exported shareable manifests do not require persisted generated IDs; derive IDs at runtime or move local-only state elsewhere if needed.
3. Preserve existing behavior for `repo add`, `repo sync`, `repo remove`, and `repo verify`.
4. Keep `source.include` round-tripping unchanged and validated through the existing pattern matcher.
5. Add or update tests for migration, backward compatibility, and save/load round-trips.

## Files to Modify
- pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go
- pkg/repomanifest/manifest_test.go
- cmd/repo_add.go and related callers if persistence changes require updates
- test/ and cmd/ repo tests that assert manifest shape

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Existing manifests that contain `id` fields still load without data loss.
- [ ] The shareable manifest no longer requires local-only/generated IDs to function.
- [ ] Include filters still validate and round-trip unchanged.
- [ ] Existing repo commands continue to work with the updated manifest model.
- [ ] Regression tests cover backward compatibility and the new saved shape.
close_reason: Implemented shareable manifest persistence: source IDs now runtime-only (not persisted), legacy id fields still load, and regression tests updated for backward compatibility and include round-tripping.

title: Implement manifest apply and merge workflow
description:
## Description
Implement the apply/merge command workflow after the CLI spec, manifest persistence model, and manifest input loading behavior are defined.

## Instructions
1. Add the `repo apply` merge path that operates on a parsed manifest model from the loader task.
2. Implement merge behavior from the approved spec, including:
   - additive merges for missing sources
   - idempotent no-op reapply for identical sources
   - conflict handling when the same source name points somewhere different
   - explicit handling of `include` filter replacement/preservation
3. Keep the existing `source.include` filter syntax; do not invent a new filtering format.
4. Support dry-run style reporting so users can preview adds, updates, no-ops, and conflicts.
5. Ensure apply can auto-initialize the local repository when needed, or fail with a clear message if a different UX is approved in the spec.
6. Add focused unit/integration tests for repeated apply, merge updates, and conflict cases.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/repo_apply.go and related repo command wiring
- pkg/repomanifest/manifest.go and/or new merge helpers
- cmd/ and test/ repo apply/merge tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `repo apply` merges a parsed manifest into the local repo manifest.
- [ ] Re-applying an identical manifest is idempotent.
- [ ] Conflicts are surfaced clearly instead of silently overwriting.
- [ ] Existing include filters are preserved exactly.
- [ ] Dry-run output reports adds, updates, no-ops, and conflicts.
- [ ] Fresh-repo behavior is implemented according to the approved CLI spec.
close_reason: Implemented repo apply merge workflow with conflict/no-op/update/add reporting, include replace/preserve handling, fresh-repo init behavior, and focused apply+merge tests

title: Fix E2E tests expecting old repo sync output
description:
## Description
Fix the failing E2E tests now blocking the release after Build run `22809895698`.

The lint blockers are resolved and the main `Test` job is green, but the `E2E Tests` job still fails. The failed log shows multiple tests no longer parse `repo sync` output correctly after the recent sync output changes.

## Observed Failures
From GitHub Actions run `22809895698`:
- `TestE2E_PruneOnCleanRepo` — sync completed but test parsed `0 resources synced`
- `TestE2E_PruneActuallyRemovesNothing` — same parsing problem
- `TestE2E_PruneOutputFormats` — same parsing problem
- `TestE2E_BasicReadOperations` — same parsing problem
- `TestE2E_SyncIdempotency` — same parsing problem (`Added=0, Updated=0` despite visible sync output)

## Likely Cause
The tests appear to rely on older `repo sync` output formatting and no longer parse the current compact summary lines correctly.

## Instructions
1. Inspect the failing E2E tests and the shared parsing helpers they use.
2. Update the tests to match the current `repo sync` output format, keeping behavior expectations intact.
3. Prefer fixing shared helpers over duplicating parsing changes in each test.
4. Run `make e2e-test` locally.
5. Run `make test` if the fix touches shared test code used elsewhere.
6. Record what changed and why.

## Files to Modify
- `test/e2e/prune_test.go`
- `test/e2e/read_operations_test.go`
- `test/e2e/sync_test.go`
- any shared E2E helper file needed for output parsing

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The failing E2E tests are updated for current sync output.
- [ ] `make e2e-test` passes.
- [ ] `make test` passes if affected helper code is shared.
- [ ] No production behavior is changed just to satisfy old test parsing assumptions.
close_reason: Completed: updated shared E2E sync output parser for compact repo sync summaries, validated with make e2e-test and make test

title: Fix remote repo cache refresh in aimgr repo sync
description:
## Description
`aimgr repo sync` reports success for remote sources even when the cached repository in `.workspace/` is stale. The sync path for remote sources calls `workspace.Manager.GetOrClone(...)` but does not call `workspace.Manager.Update(...)`, so an existing cache can be reused without fetching new upstream commits.

This makes `last_synced` misleading for remote sources and can leave installed resources on an old upstream version after a successful sync.

## Evidence
- `cmd/repo_sync.go` calls `wsMgr.GetOrClone(cloneURL, src.Ref)` and then imports directly from the returned path.
- `cmd/repo_add.go` uses the safer pattern: `GetOrClone(...)` followed by `Update(...)`.
- Reproduced with the `anthropics` source: `aimgr repo sync anthropics` completed successfully, but `skill/skill-creator` remained on an older cached version instead of the newer upstream version.

## Expected Behavior
When syncing a remote source, aimgr should refresh the cached repository before importing resources so the sync reflects the current upstream state for the requested ref.

## Actual Behavior
If the remote repository already exists in `.workspace/`, `aimgr repo sync` can import from the stale cache without fetching updates.

## Likely Fix
Update the remote-source sync path in `cmd/repo_sync.go` to mirror `repo add` behavior:
1. Call `GetOrClone(...)`.
2. Call `Update(...)` for the same clone URL and ref.
3. Import from the refreshed cache path.
4. Decide and document the fallback behavior if `Update(...)` fails (hard fail vs warning + stale cache).

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/repo_sync.go` — refresh remote workspace cache during sync
- `cmd/repo_sync_test.go` — add regression coverage for stale cached remote repositories

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr repo sync` refreshes cached remote repositories before importing resources
- [ ] A regression test fails on the old behavior and passes with the fix
- [ ] Sync behavior for remote sources is consistent with `repo add` cache-refresh behavior
- [ ] The issue reproducer no longer leaves remote resources stale after a successful sync
close_reason: Fixed and tested

title: Migrate module path from hk9890 to dynatrace-oss
description:
## Description
The GitHub repo has moved from `github.com/hk9890/ai-config-manager` to `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager`. All references to the old org need to be updated.

GitHub currently redirects, but redirects are fragile and the old path looks unprofessional.

## Scope
- **139 files**, **383 occurrences** of `hk9890/ai-config-manager`
- Go module path (`go.mod` + 119 `.go` files with import paths)
- Build config (`.goreleaser.yaml`, `Makefile`)

## Risks
- **Breaking change for Go consumers**: Anyone doing `go install github.com/hk9890/...` will need the new path (but GitHub redirect covers transitional period)
- **CI/CD**: GoReleaser ldflags reference the module path
- **Beads issues.jsonl**: Contains old paths in closed issues — leave as historical record

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `go build ./...` succeeds
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] `go install github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/cmd/aimgr@latest` works

title: Show installed packages in aimgr list output
description:
## Summary

`aimgr list` currently only shows installed commands, skills, and agents (discovered via symlinks). It does NOT show packages, even though `ai.package.yaml` can contain `package/` entries (e.g., `package/opencode-coder`). Users need to see installed packages in the list output.

## Current Behavior

- `aimgr list` calls `installer.List()` which scans for symlinks only → finds commands, skills, agents
- Packages are never included because they have no symlinks (they are expanded into constituent resources at install time)
- `ai.package.yaml` already tracks `package/` entries correctly (install writes them)
- `expandManifestResources()` already knows how to expand packages for sync status computation
- Pattern matching (`pkg/pattern/matcher.go`) already recognizes `package` type at line 103-104

## Required Changes

All changes are in `cmd/list_installed.go` unless noted:

### 1. Inject package entries into the resource list (in `RunE`, after `installer.List()`)
- Read `ai.package.yaml` for `package/` entries
- For each package ref, load the package definition from the repo via `resource.LoadPackage()`
- Create `resource.Resource` entries with `Type: resource.PackageType` and append to the resources list
- This must happen BEFORE pattern filtering (line 125) so `aimgr list "package/*"` works

### 2. Add packages bucket in `outputInstalledTable()` (lines 352-445)
- Add a `packages` slice alongside `commands`, `skills`, `agents` in the switch (lines 354-367)
- Add a rendering section for packages after agents, with separator logic
- For TARGETS column: show resource count from the package definition (e.g., `5 resources`) since packages have no symlinks
- For STATUS column: show `✓` (packages do not have symlink health to check)
- For SYNC column: use existing `formatSyncStatus()` with `isInstalled=true` (if it is in the manifest, it is installed)

### 3. Handle packages in `buildResourceInfo()` (lines 271-315)
- `isInstalledInTool()` returns false for packages (default case at line 339) — this is correct
- For packages, set Targets to something meaningful (resource count string) or leave empty and handle in table rendering
- Consider packages always "installed" for sync status purposes (they exist in the manifest)

### 4. Handle JSON/YAML output
- `outputInstalledJSON()` and `outputInstalledYAML()` should work automatically since packages will be in the `[]ResourceInfo` slice — verify this works

## Testing

- Run `go build ./...` to verify compilation
- Run `go test ./cmd/ -run TestList -v` to run existing list tests
- Run `go test ./... -count=1` for full test suite
- Manual verification: run `./aimgr list` in this project dir and confirm packages appear
- Manual verification: run `./aimgr list "package/*"` and confirm filtering works

## Acceptance Criteria

- `aimgr list` shows packages from `ai.package.yaml` alongside commands/skills/agents
- Packages are shown with resource count, sync status, and a description
- `aimgr list "package/*"` filters to only packages
- JSON and YAML output includes packages
- All existing tests pass
- No regressions in existing list behavior
close_reason: done

title: lucb.1 missing verify, repair, clean, init, and ai.package.yaml concepts
description:
## Bug

lucb.1 acceptance criteria say 'Doc covers full install/uninstall workflow' but the instructions don't mention:

- `aimgr verify` — Read-only check of project installation health
- `aimgr repair` — Fix broken installations (broken symlinks, missing resources, unmanaged files, prune-package)
- `aimgr clean` — Remove ALL installed resources from project
- `aimgr init` — Initialize ai.package.yaml in a project
- `ai.package.yaml` manifest concept — `aimgr install` with no args installs from manifest
- `--save / --no-save` flags on install/uninstall
- `--project-path` flag

These are core to the install/uninstall lifecycle. There's an entire docs/user-guide/repair.md dedicated to verify+repair.

## Impact
The resulting install-uninstall.md will be missing significant functionality that users need for day-to-day project management.

## Fix
Add these to lucb.1 instructions:
- Verify installation health section
- Repair broken installations section  
- Clean all resources section
- Initialize project manifest section
- Manifest-based install (`aimgr install` with no args)
close_reason: Fixed in install-uninstall.md — covers verify, repair, clean, init, ai.package.yaml manifest, --save/--no-save flags, and manifest-based install.

title: lucb.2 missing 7+ repo subcommands (init, info, describe, verify, repair, drop, prune)
description:
## Bug

lucb.2 instructions say to cover 'Adding sources, Syncing, Removing resources, Validating, CI/CD, Troubleshooting' — but the actual CLI has 10 repo subcommands and the plan only covers 3 (add, sync, remove).

Missing from lucb.2 instructions:
- `aimgr repo init` — Initialize repository (required first step for new users)
- `aimgr repo info` — Display repository information and statistics
- `aimgr repo describe` — Display detailed resource information
- `aimgr repo verify` — Check repository metadata and package integrity
- `aimgr repo repair` — Fix repository metadata issues
- `aimgr repo drop` — Remove all resources from repository (nuclear option)
- `aimgr repo prune` — Remove unreferenced workspace caches

## Impact
The resulting manage-repository.md will be incomplete, missing half the available commands. Agents won't know about `repo init`, `repo info`, `repo describe`, etc.

## Fix
Add these commands to lucb.2 instructions and acceptance criteria. At minimum, repo init, repo info, repo describe, repo verify, and repo repair should be covered.
close_reason: Fixed in manage-repository.md — all repo subcommands documented: init, add, sync, remove, list, describe, info, verify, repair, drop, prune.

title: Fix repo remove semantics — it removes sources not individual resources
description:
## Bug

lucb.2 instructions say 'Removing resources from repo (with safety warnings)' and the current SKILL.md shows `aimgr repo remove skill/name`. But `aimgr repo remove` is actually **source removal** (symmetrical with `repo add`). It removes a source entry from ai.repo.yaml and its orphaned resources — NOT individual resources by type/name pattern.

CLI help: 'Remove a source from the repository (symmetrical with repo add). This command operates on SOURCES (not individual resources).'

Correct usage: `aimgr repo remove <source-name|path|url>`

## Impact
If the new docs perpetuate this error, agents will run the wrong command and potentially delete entire sources when the user wanted to remove one resource.

## Fix
Update lucb.2 instructions to:
1. Document `repo remove` as source removal (not individual resource deletion)
2. Document that individual resource removal is done by modifying the source and running `repo sync`
3. Include `--keep-resources` flag documentation
4. Fix the example syntax throughout
close_reason: Fixed in install-uninstall.md and manage-repository.md — repo remove correctly documented as source removal, not individual resource deletion.

title: Create references/discover-resources.md
description:
## Description
Create a new reference doc for Use Case 3: Discover Useful Resources. This is a brand new use case where the agent scans the project and recommends skills/packages that would be useful.

## Instructions
1. Create file `ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/discover-resources.md`
2. Define the discovery workflow:
   - Agent scans project for signals (package.json, go.mod, Dockerfile, .github/workflows/, Makefile, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, tsconfig.json, etc.)
   - Agent queries available resources: `aimgr repo list --format=json`
   - Agent matches project signals to resource descriptions/tags
   - Agent presents recommendations with rationale
   - User picks which to install
   - Agent installs chosen resources
3. Define signal-to-resource mapping examples:
   - Go project → skill/go-testing, skill/go-linting
   - Node project → skill/node-testing
   - Docker present → skill/docker-ops
   - GitHub Actions → skill/ci-cd
   - PDF files → skill/pdf-processing
   - etc. (these are illustrative — actual matching uses repo list descriptions)
4. The matching should be dynamic based on what's in the repo, not hardcoded
5. Include a "scan checklist" of files/dirs to look for
6. Do NOT use relative links to docs/ in this repo

## Files to Modify
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/discover-resources.md (create)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Clear workflow: scan → query → match → recommend → install
- [ ] Scan checklist of project signals defined
- [ ] Matching is described as dynamic (based on repo list), not hardcoded
- [ ] Example signal-to-resource mappings for illustration
- [ ] Installs go through the standard install flow (with restart reminder)
- [ ] No relative links to this repo's docs/

title: Scaffold aimgr repo repair command
description:
## Description

Create the `aimgr repo repair` command that replaces `repo verify --fix`. This fixes repository metadata issues: creates missing metadata for resources, removes orphaned metadata files.

## Context

The existing fix logic lives in `cmd/repo_verify.go` in the `--fix` handling block. The repo verify command checks for: resources without metadata, type mismatches, metadata with non-existent sources, orphaned metadata, and packages with missing resource references.

## Instructions

1. Create `cmd/repo_repair.go` with a new `repoRepairCmd` registered on `repoCmd`
2. The command should:
   - Run the same diagnostic scan as `repo verify` (`verifyRepository()` or equivalent)
   - Apply fixes for each fixable issue:
     - Resources without metadata: create metadata via `createMetadataForResource()`
     - Orphaned metadata (source file gone): remove metadata JSON file
   - Report unfixable issues (type mismatches, missing package refs) with guidance
   - Support `--format` flag (text/json) for structured output
3. Add `--dry-run` flag: show what would be fixed without changing anything
4. Handle packages in the repo: validate package resource references properly

## Files to Modify

- `cmd/repo_repair.go` — CREATE: new repo repair command
- `cmd/repo.go` — may need to verify subcommand registration (init() in repo_repair.go handles this)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `aimgr repo repair` fixes missing metadata and removes orphaned metadata
- [ ] `aimgr repo repair --dry-run` shows planned changes without applying
- [ ] Unfixable issues (type mismatches) are reported with guidance
- [ ] Package resource validation works correctly
- [ ] Output supports text and JSON formats
- [ ] Unit tests cover: missing metadata fix, orphaned metadata removal, dry-run mode, no-issues case
- [ ] Tests use existing patterns: `t.TempDir()`, `repo.NewManagerWithPath()`, metadata fixtures
- [ ] `go build ./...` passes
- [ ] `go test ./cmd/ -run TestRepoRepair` passes
close_reason: Implemented cmd/repo_repair.go with 8 passing tests

title: Scaffold aimgr repair command with base repair logic
description:
## Description

Create the `aimgr repair` command that replaces `verify --fix`. This task implements the safe default behavior: fix broken symlinks, reinstall wrong-repo symlinks, and install resources listed in manifest but missing on disk.

## Context

The existing fix logic lives in `cmd/project_verify.go` in `fixVerifyIssues()`. This task extracts and refactors that logic into a new `cmd/repair.go` command. The repair command reuses the existing `scanProjectIssues()` and `checkManifestSync()` diagnostic functions from verify, then applies fixes.

## Instructions

1. Create `cmd/repair.go` with a new `repairCmd` registered on `rootCmd`
2. The command should:
   - Run the same diagnostic scan as verify (`scanProjectIssues` + `checkManifestSync`)
   - Apply fixes for each issue type:
     - `broken` / `wrong-repo`: remove broken symlink, reinstall from repo
     - `not-installed`: install from repo (parse type/name from resource reference)
     - `orphaned`: print hint to use `aimgr uninstall` (same as today — do NOT auto-remove)
   - Support `--format` flag (text/json) for structured output
3. Handle **packages** properly: when manifest has `package/foo`, expand to constituent resources via `manager.GetPackage()` before checking installation
4. Handle **hierarchical commands**: when reinstalling commands like `namespace/command`, ensure parent directories are created (existing `InstallCommand` already does this via `os.MkdirAll`)
5. Handle **multiple tools**: iterate detected tools same as verify does today
6. Add flag stubs for `--reset`, `--prune-package`, `--force`, `--dry-run` (no implementation yet, just flag definitions so the CLI surface is established)

## Files to Modify

- `cmd/repair.go` — CREATE: new repair command
- `cmd/root.go` — may need to verify command registration (init() in repair.go handles this)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `aimgr repair` runs and fixes broken symlinks, missing resources
- [ ] `aimgr repair --help` shows all planned flags (even if some are no-ops yet)
- [ ] Package resources are correctly expanded when checking installation
- [ ] Hierarchical command `namespace/command` can be reinstalled correctly
- [ ] Output matches verify --fix style (text and JSON formats)
- [ ] Unit tests cover: basic repair, package expansion, hierarchical commands, no-issues case
- [ ] Tests use existing patterns: `t.TempDir()`, real filesystem structures, `repo.NewManagerWithPath()`, `install.NewInstaller()`
- [ ] `go build ./...` passes
- [ ] `go test ./cmd/ -run TestRepair` passes
close_reason: Implemented cmd/repair.go with 9 passing tests

title: Add aimgr repair and aimgr repo repair commands
description:
## Description

Replace the overloaded `verify --fix` pattern with dedicated `repair` commands that have clear, composable flags for different repair strategies.

## Current State

- `aimgr verify --fix` combines read-only diagnostics with write actions
- No way to remove unmanaged files from resource directories
- No way to clean stale references from `ai.package.yaml`
- `aimgr repo verify --fix` has the same read/write conflation

## Goals

- `aimgr repair` — fix broken symlinks, install missing resources (safe default)
- `aimgr repair --reset` — above + remove unmanaged files (interactive, like git reset)
- `aimgr repair --prune-package` — above + remove invalid entries from ai.package.yaml (smart escalation: suggest repo sync → repo repair → remove)
- `aimgr repair --force` — skip all interactive prompts on destructive actions
- `aimgr repair --dry-run` — show what would happen without changing anything
- `aimgr repo repair` — fix repo metadata issues (with --dry-run support)
- Deprecate `verify --fix` with notice pointing to `repair`

## Edge Cases to Handle

- **Packages**: manifest entries like `package/foo` must be expanded to constituent resources when checking installation and when building the "managed" set for --reset
- **Hierarchical commands**: `namespace/command.md` stored as subdirectories — repair scanning must handle one-level recursion matching existing verify patterns
- **Combined flags**: `--reset --prune-package --force` must compose correctly
- **Multiple tool targets**: repair operates across all detected tools (Claude, OpenCode, etc.)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All child tasks completed
- [ ] Acceptance gate passed
- [ ] `verify --fix` deprecated with clear migration message
notes:
## Epic Complete Summary

### What was built

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| aimgr repair | Fix broken symlinks, install missing resources (safe) |
| aimgr repair --reset | Above + remove unmanaged files (interactive, --force to skip prompts) |
| aimgr repair --prune-package | Above + remove invalid ai.package.yaml entries (smart escalation: repo sync, repo repair, remove) |
| aimgr repair --dry-run | Show what would happen without changes |
| aimgr repo repair | Fix repo metadata (missing metadata, orphaned files) |
| aimgr repo repair --dry-run | Show what would be fixed |
| verify --fix | Now deprecated with warning pointing to repair |

### Test coverage
- 35 unit tests across TestRepair*, TestRepairReset*, TestRepairPrunePackage*, TestRepoRepair*
- 23 integration tests covering packages, hierarchical commands, combined flags, JSON output
- All passing, no regressions

### Files created/modified
- cmd/repair.go — new repair command
- cmd/repo_repair.go — new repo repair command
- cmd/repair_test.go — unit tests
- cmd/repo_repair_test.go — unit tests
- cmd/project_verify.go — deprecation warning
- cmd/repo_verify.go — deprecation warning
- test/repair_test.go — integration tests
- test/repo_repair_test.go — integration tests
- test/repair_helpers.go — test helpers

### Pre-existing bugs filed (to address separately)
- ai-config-manager-qu6m / ai-config-manager-7co2 / ai-config-manager-lft2 — pre-existing TestRepoVerify* failures
- ai-config-manager-6al4 — pre-existing TestVerifyRepository_TypeMismatch failure

title: Create GitHub Release v2.8.0
description:
## Description
Create and push the v2.8.0 tag to trigger the GitHub Actions release workflow.

This project uses **tag-triggered GitHub Actions**. Do NOT use `gh workflow run`.

## Instructions

### 1. Create annotated tag
```bash
git tag -a v2.8.0 -m "Release v2.8.0"
```

### 2. Push tag (triggers GoReleaser workflow)
```bash
git push origin v2.8.0
```

### 3. Monitor the workflow
```bash
gh run watch
```
The workflow builds binaries, creates archives, generates checksums, and publishes the GitHub release via GoReleaser.

### 4. Verify release exists
```bash
gh release view v2.8.0
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tag v2.8.0 created and pushed
- [ ] GoReleaser workflow completed successfully
- [ ] GitHub release visible with binary assets
- [ ] Release has binaries for linux/darwin/windows (amd64/arm64)

## STOP if workflow fails
Report to user, do NOT proceed.
close_reason: Tag v2.8.0 pushed, GoReleaser workflow completed in 2m9s, release published with 7 assets (6 platform binaries + checksums)

title: Update CHANGELOG for v2.8.0
description:
## Description
Update CHANGELOG.md with the v2.8.0 entry and commit it.

## Instructions

### 1. Analyze commits since v2.7.0
```bash
git log v2.7.0..HEAD --oneline
```

### 2. Add CHANGELOG entry
Add a new section at the top of CHANGELOG.md (after the header), following Keep a Changelog format:

```markdown
## [2.8.0] - 2026-03-01

### Fixed
- **Orphaned metadata cleanup** — `repo remove` now handles cases where resource file is gone but metadata persists, preventing "resource not found" errors during sync cleanup
- **Verify subdirectory scanning** — `verify` now scans subdirectories for nested commands and detects orphaned resources
- **List legend labels** — Improved list legend labels and shortened broken status display
- **Package verify output** — Named missing resources in package verify output
- **Command references** — Corrected 'aimgr project verify' references to 'aimgr verify' in sync/remove warning messages
- **Test fixes** — Corrected TestListEnhanced tests to use listInstalledCmd
```

### 3. Commit and push
```bash
git add CHANGELOG.md
git commit -m "docs: update CHANGELOG for v2.8.0"
git push origin main
```

## Files to Modify
- CHANGELOG.md — Add v2.8.0 entry at top

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md has v2.8.0 entry with today's date (2026-03-01)
- [ ] All changes since v2.7.0 documented
- [ ] Follows Keep a Changelog format
- [ ] Committed and pushed to main
close_reason: CHANGELOG updated with v2.8.0 entry (f342c43) and pushed to main

title: Quality Gates for v2.8.0
description:
## Description
Run all quality gates before proceeding with the release.

## Instructions

### 1. Verify clean working tree
```bash
git status
```
Only .beads/ files should be modified.

### 2. Run all tests
```bash
make test
```
This runs: go vet, unit tests, and integration tests. ALL must pass with ZERO failures.

### 3. Build the binary
```bash
make build
```
Must succeed without errors.

### 4. Check CI status
```bash
gh run list --limit 1
```
Latest commit should be green.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Clean working tree (no uncommitted code changes)
- [ ] All tests pass (ZERO failures): `make test`
- [ ] Build succeeds: `make build`
- [ ] CI green (latest commit passes): `gh run list --limit 1`

## STOP if ANY fails
Report to user, do NOT proceed to next phase.
close_reason: All quality gates passed: 957 tests/0 failures, build succeeds. CI lint warnings are pre-existing (tracked in t5dx)

title: Sync/remove suggest non-existent 'aimgr project verify' command; should be 'aimgr verify'
description:
## Description

After `aimgr repo sync` or `aimgr repo remove` removes resources, the warning message tells users:

```
Run 'aimgr project verify --fix' in affected projects to clean up broken symlinks.
```

But `aimgr project` doesn't exist. The actual command is `aimgr verify --fix`.

## Root Cause

Three locations reference the wrong command name:

1. `cmd/repo_sync.go:379` — `"aimgr project verify --fix"`
2. `cmd/repo_remove.go:248` — `"aimgr project verify --fix"` 
3. `cmd/repo_remove.go:240` — comment says `'aimgr project verify'`
4. `cmd/repo_remove_test.go:1042-1043` — test checks for `"project verify"` string

## Instructions

1. In `cmd/repo_sync.go` line 379: Change `"aimgr project verify --fix"` to `"aimgr verify --fix"`
2. In `cmd/repo_remove.go` line 248: Change `"aimgr project verify --fix"` to `"aimgr verify --fix"` 
3. In `cmd/repo_remove.go` line 240: Update comment to say `'aimgr verify'`
4. In `cmd/repo_remove_test.go` lines 1042-1043: Update test assertion from `"project verify"` to just `"verify"` — but make sure the assertion is specific enough to not match random occurrences of "verify"

## Files to Modify

- `cmd/repo_sync.go` — Fix error message (line 379)
- `cmd/repo_remove.go` — Fix error message (line 248) and comment (line 240)
- `cmd/repo_remove_test.go` — Fix test assertion (lines 1042-1043)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `aimgr repo sync` output references `aimgr verify --fix` (not `aimgr project verify --fix`)
- [ ] `aimgr repo remove` output references `aimgr verify --fix` (not `aimgr project verify --fix`)
- [ ] Test still passes with corrected assertion
- [ ] All tests pass: `make test`
close_reason: Fixed: all 3 occurrences of 'aimgr project verify' changed to 'aimgr verify' in repo_sync.go, repo_remove.go, and the test.

title: Sync fails to remove orphaned resources when resource file is missing but metadata exists
close_reason: Fixed: Remove() now tracks resourceExists as a bool instead of early-returning. Metadata cleanup always proceeds. Returns not-found only when neither file nor metadata existed. 4 new tests added, all passing.

title: verify doesn't scan subdirectories — misses broken nested commands
description:
## Description

`aimgr verify` fails to detect broken symlinks in nested/namespaced command directories. When a command like `opencode-coder/init` has a broken symlink, `verify` reports "✓ All installed resources are valid" while `list` correctly shows it as broken (`✗ br…`).

## Root Cause

`verifyDirectory()` in `cmd/project_verify.go` (line 201-267) only scans top-level entries in tool directories. When it encounters a subdirectory (like `opencode-coder/` inside `.opencode/commands/`), it checks if it's a symlink, finds it isn't, and skips it. It never recurses into subdirectories.

Meanwhile, `scanFileSymlinks()` in `pkg/install/installer.go` (lines 458-501) properly handles nested commands by iterating into subdirectories.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a nested command installed with a broken symlink target (e.g., `command/opencode-coder/init`)
2. Run `aimgr list` — shows the broken resource with `✗` status and prints "⚠ 1 broken resource(s) found"
3. Run `aimgr verify` — incorrectly reports "✓ All installed resources are valid"

## Expected Behavior

`aimgr verify` should detect broken symlinks in nested command directories, consistent with how `aimgr list` detects them.

## Instructions

1. Modify `verifyDirectory()` in `cmd/project_verify.go` to recurse into subdirectories
2. When a non-symlink directory entry is found, scan its entries for symlinks
3. Use the same pattern as `scanFileSymlinks()` in `pkg/install/installer.go` lines 458-501
4. For nested resources, construct the resource name as `dirname/filename-without-ext` (matching list behavior)
5. Apply the same broken/wrong-repo checks to nested symlinks

## Files to Modify

- `cmd/project_verify.go` — Update `verifyDirectory()` to handle nested directories
- `cmd/project_verify_test.go` — Add tests for broken nested command detection

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `aimgr verify` detects broken symlinks inside nested command directories
- [ ] `aimgr verify --fix` can fix broken nested command symlinks
- [ ] Existing verify tests still pass
- [ ] New test covers nested command directory scanning
- [ ] `aimgr list` and `aimgr verify` report consistent results for broken resources
close_reason: Fixed: verifyDirectory() now recurses one level into subdirectories to detect broken/wrong-repo nested command symlinks. Extracted shared verifySymlink() helper. Added 11 new tests covering nested detection, wrong-repo, depth limiting, and mixed scenarios. All tests pass.

title: package verify says 'not all resources installed' without naming which ones are missing
description:
## Description
\`aimgr verify\` reports packages as "not all package resources are installed" without naming WHICH resources are missing. The user has no idea what to do.

Example output:
\`\`\`
package/opencode-coder │ any │ ⚠ not-insta… │ Listed in ai.package.yaml but not all package resources are installed
\`\`\`

This gives zero actionable information. The user has to guess which of the package's 11 resources is missing.

## Root Cause
In \`cmd/project_verify.go\` lines 277-345, the code iterates each resource in the package, checks if it's installed, but on failure only reports a single generic message at the package level (line 342):

\`\`\`go
Description: fmt.Sprintf("Listed in %s but not all package resources are installed", manifest.ManifestFileName),
\`\`\`

It doesn't collect or report which specific resources failed the check.

## Fix
Track which resources are missing during the loop (lines 278-334) and include them in the issue description. E.g.:
\`\`\`
package/opencode-coder: 5 resources not installed: agent/orchestrator, agent/reviewer, agent/tasker, agent/verifier, command/opencode-coder/dump-session
\`\`\`

## Files to Modify
- \`cmd/project_verify.go\` — lines 277-345: collect missing resource names and include them in the VerifyIssue description

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Verify output names each missing resource in a package
- [ ] Message is actionable — user knows exactly which resources to install
- [ ] Existing verify tests updated for new message format
- [ ] \`make test\` passes
close_reason: Fixed: verify now lists each missing resource by name. Added missingResources collector and 4 new tests. make test passes.

title: verify misses broken symlinks - uses os.Lstat instead of os.Stat
description:
## Description
\`aimgr verify\` does not report broken symlinks as issues. A resource with a broken symlink is simply invisible to verify — it passes the check silently.

The user has a broken symlink, runs \`verify\` to understand the problem, and gets nothing about it.

## Root Cause
\`checkManifestSync()\` in \`cmd/project_verify.go\` uses \`os.Lstat()\` to check if resources are installed:

- **Line 320** (package resources): \`os.Lstat(path)\` — does NOT follow symlinks
- **Line 376** (regular resources): \`os.Lstat(dir)\` — does NOT follow symlinks

\`os.Lstat()\` checks if the symlink node exists on disk, not whether its target exists. A broken symlink (target deleted) still passes this check and is considered "installed".

Meanwhile, \`verifyDirectory()\` (Phase 1, line 207) correctly uses \`os.Stat()\` which follows symlinks and detects broken targets. But Phase 1 may not cover all resource types or the results don't surface properly.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Install a resource via \`aimgr install\` (creates symlink)
2. Delete or move the symlink target in the repo
3. Run \`aimgr verify\`
4. Expected: issue reported for broken symlink
5. Actual: broken symlink not reported

## Fix
Change \`os.Lstat()\` to \`os.Stat()\` at lines 320 and 376 of \`cmd/project_verify.go\` to match the behavior of \`verifyDirectory()\` at line 207.

Consider deduplication: if Phase 1 already reports "broken" for the individual symlink, Phase 2 should either skip it or report it differently at the package level.

## Files to Modify
- \`cmd/project_verify.go\` — lines 320, 376: change \`os.Lstat()\` to \`os.Stat()\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] \`aimgr verify\` reports broken symlinks as specific issues
- [ ] Broken symlink in a package resource is correctly detected
- [ ] No duplicate issues between Phase 1 and Phase 2
- [ ] Existing tests updated, new test for broken symlink case
- [ ] \`make test\` passes
close_reason: Fixed: os.Lstat→os.Stat at lines 320/376, added deduplicateIssues(), fixed pre-existing test bugs, added 3 new tests. make test passes.
